options.text: strictly increasing on R
options.html: strictly increasing on R
options.explanation: To determine the behavior of the function {tex}f(x)=x^3-3 x^2+12 x-18{/tex} , we first compute its derivative: {tex} f^{\prime}(x)=3 x^2-6 x+12 \text {. }{/tex} Next, we analyze the derivative to find critical points and its sign: Finding the roots of {tex}f^{\prime}(x){/tex} : {tex} f^{\prime}(x)=3\left(x^2-2 x+4\right){/tex} The discriminant of the quadratic {tex}x^2-2 x+4{/tex} is: {tex} D=(-2)^2-4 \cdot 1 \cdot 4=4-16=-12{/tex} Since the discriminant is negative, {tex}f^{\prime}(x){/tex} has no real roots, which means it does not change sign. Analyzing the sign of {tex}f^{\prime}(x){/tex} : The quadratic x 2 - 2x + 4 opens upwards (as the coefficient of x 2 is positive) and is always positive because it has no real roots. Therefore, {tex}f^{\prime}(x){/tex} > 0 for all x. Since {tex}f^{\prime}(x)>0{/tex} for all {tex}x \in R{/tex} , the function {tex}f(x){/tex} is strictly increasing on the entire real line. Thus, the correct answer is strictly increasing on R

R(x) = 3x2 + 36x + 5. The marginal revenue, when x = 15 is
question_text: The total revenue in Rupees received from the sale of x units of a product is given by R(x) = 3x 2 + 36x + 5. The marginal revenue, when x = 15 is

options.html: 116
96
90
126
options.explanation: Marginal revenue (MR) is the rate of change of total revenue with respect to the number of units sold. So, MR = {tex}\frac{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{R}}{\mathrm{d} \mathrm{x}}{/tex} = 6x + 36 = 6x + 36 {tex}\therefore{/tex} when x = 15, then MR = 6(15) + 36 = 126 Therefore, the marginal revenue when x = 15 is 126.

Now, the point x = 2 divides the real line into two disjoint intervals namely, ( {tex}-\infty{/tex} , 2) and (2, {tex}\infty{/tex} ). In the interval ( {tex}-\infty{/tex} , 2), f'(x) = 2x - 4 < 0. question_html: The volume of a cube is increasing at the rate of 8 cm3/sec. How fast is the surface area increasing when the length of an edge is 12 cm?
question_text: The volume of a cube is increasing at the rate of 8 cm 3 /sec. How fast is the surface area increasing when the length of an edge is 12 cm?
explanation: Let x cm be the edge of the cube and y be the surface area of the cube at any time t Given: Rate of increase of volume of cube {tex} = 8c{m^3}/\sec {/tex} {tex} \frac{d}{{dt}}\left( {{x^3}} \right){/tex} = 8 {tex} \Rightarrow 3{x^2}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 8{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = \frac{8}{{3{x^2}}}{/tex} ...(i) {tex}y = 6{x^2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Rate of change of surface area of the cube {tex}= \frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 6\frac{d}{{dt}}{x^2}{/tex} {tex}= 6\left( {2x\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}} \right){/tex} {tex} = 12x\left( {\frac{8}{{3{x^2}}}} \right){/tex} [from (1)] {tex}= 4\left( {\frac{8}{x}} \right) = \frac{{32}}{x}c{m^2}/\sec{/tex} Putting x = 12 cm (given), {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = \frac{{32}}{{12}} = \frac{8}{3}c{m^2}\sec {/tex} Since {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex} is positive, therefore surface area is increasing at the rate of {tex}\frac{8}{3}c{m^2}/\sec {/tex}
solution: Let x cm be the edge of the cube and y be the surface area of the cube at any time t Given: Rate of increase of volume of cube {tex} = 8c{m^3}/\sec {/tex} {tex} \frac{d}{{dt}}\left( {{x^3}} \right){/tex} = 8 {tex} \Rightarrow 3{x^2}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 8{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = \frac{8}{{3{x^2}}}{/tex} ...(i) {tex}y = 6{x^2}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Rate of change of surface area of the cube {tex}= \frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 6\frac{d}{{dt}}{x^2}{/tex} {tex}= 6\left( {2x\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}} \right){/tex} {tex} = 12x\left( {\frac{8}{{3{x^2}}}} \right){/tex} [from (1)] {tex}= 4\left( {\frac{8}{x}} \right) = \frac{{32}}{x}c{m^2}/\sec{/tex} Putting x = 12 cm (given), {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = \frac{{32}}{{12}} = \frac{8}{3}c{m^2}\sec {/tex} Since {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex} is positive, therefore surface area is increasing at the rate of {tex}\frac{8}{3}c{m^2}/\sec {/tex}
solution_html:
Let x cm be the edge of the cube and y be the surface area of the cube at any time t

Given: Rate of increase of volume of cube {tex} = 8c{m^3}/\sec {/tex}

{tex} \frac{d}{{dt}}\left( {{x^3}} \right){/tex} = 8             

{tex} \Rightarrow 3{x^2}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 8{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = \frac{8}{{3{x^2}}}{/tex} ...(i)

{tex}y = 6{x^2}{/tex}

{tex}\therefore{/tex}  Rate of change of surface area of the cube {tex}= \frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 6\frac{d}{{dt}}{x^2}{/tex} 

{tex}= 6\left( {2x\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}} \right){/tex}

{tex} = 12x\left( {\frac{8}{{3{x^2}}}} \right){/tex} [from (1)]

{tex}= 4\left( {\frac{8}{x}} \right) = \frac{{32}}{x}c{m^2}/\sec{/tex}

Putting x = 12 cm (given),

{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = \frac{{32}}{{12}} = \frac{8}{3}c{m^2}\sec {/tex}

Since {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex}  is positive, therefore surface area is increasing at the rate of {tex}\frac{8}{3}c{m^2}/\sec {/tex}

question_html: Find the interval in function -2x3 - 9x2 - 12x + 1 is increasing or decreasing:
question_text: Find the interval in function -2x 3 - 9x 2 - 12x + 1 is increasing or decreasing:
explanation: It is given that function f(x) = -2x 3 - 9x 2 - 12x + 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f '(x) = -6x 2 - 18x - 12 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f '(x) = -6(x 2 + 3x + 2) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f '(x) = -6(x + 1)(x + 2) If f '(x) = 0, then we get, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -1 and -2 So, the points x = -1 and x = -2 divide the real line into three disjoint intervals, {tex}(-\infty,-2),(-2,-1){/tex} and {tex}(-1, \infty){/tex} So, in intervals {tex}(-\infty,-2),(-1, \infty){/tex} f '(x) = -6(x + 1)(x + 2) < 0 Therefore, the given function 'f ' is strictly decreasing for x < -2 and x > -1 Further, in interval (-2, -1) f '(x) = -6(x + 1)(x + 2) > 0 Therefore, the given function (f) is strictly increasing for -2 < x < -1
solution: It is given that function f(x) = -2x 3 - 9x 2 - 12x + 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f '(x) = -6x 2 - 18x - 12 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f '(x) = -6(x 2 + 3x + 2) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f '(x) = -6(x + 1)(x + 2) If f '(x) = 0, then we get, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -1 and -2 So, the points x = -1 and x = -2 divide the real line into three disjoint intervals, {tex}(-\infty,-2),(-2,-1){/tex} and {tex}(-1, \infty){/tex} So, in intervals {tex}(-\infty,-2),(-1, \infty){/tex} f '(x) = -6(x + 1)(x + 2) < 0 Therefore, the given function 'f ' is strictly decreasing for x < -2 and x > -1 Further, in interval (-2, -1) f '(x) = -6(x + 1)(x + 2) > 0 Therefore, the given function (f) is strictly increasing for -2 < x < -1
solution_html:
It is given that function f(x) = -2x3 - 9x2 - 12x + 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f '(x) = -6x2 - 18x - 12
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f '(x) = -6(x2 + 3x + 2)
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} f '(x) = -6(x + 1)(x + 2)
If f '(x) = 0, then we get,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -1 and -2
So, the points x = -1 and x = -2 divide the real line into three disjoint intervals, {tex}(-\infty,-2),(-2,-1){/tex} and {tex}(-1, \infty){/tex} 
So, in intervals {tex}(-\infty,-2),(-1, \infty){/tex}
f '(x) = -6(x + 1)(x + 2) < 0
Therefore, the given function 'f ' is strictly decreasing for x < -2 and x > -1
Further, in interval (-2, -1)
f '(x) = -6(x + 1)(x + 2) > 0
Therefore, the given function (f) is strictly increasing for -2 < x < -1

Find the absolute maximum and minimum values of a function f given by
f (x) = 2x3 – 15x2 + 36x +1 on the interval [1, 5].
question_text: Find the absolute maximum and minimum values of a function f given by f (x) = 2x 3 – 15x 2 + 36x +1 on the interval [1, 5].
explanation: We have f(x) = 2x 3 – 15x 2 + 36x + 1 or f ′(x) = 6x 2 – 30x + 36 = 6 (x – 3) (x – 2) Note that f ′(x) = 0 gives x = 2 and x = 3. We shall now evaluate the value of f at these points and at the end points of the interval [1, 5], i.e., at x = 1, x = 2, x = 3 and at x = 5. So f(1) = 2(1 3 ) – 15(1 2 ) + 36 (1) + 1 = 24 f(2) = 2(2 3 ) – 15(2 2 ) + 36 (2) + 1 = 29 f(3) = 2(3 3 ) – 15(3 2 ) + 36 (3) + 1 = 28 f(5) = 2(5 3 ) – 15(5 2 ) + 36 (5) + 1 = 56 Thus, we conclude that absolute maximum value of f on [1, 5] is 56, occurring at x =5, and absolute minimum value of f on [1, 5] is 24 which occurs at x = 1.

Note that f ′(x) = 0 gives x = 2 and x = 3.
We shall now evaluate the value of f at these points and at the end points of the interval [1, 5], i.e., at x = 1, x = 2, x = 3 and at x = 5. So
f(1) = 2(13) – 15(12) + 36 (1) + 1 = 24
f(2) = 2(23) – 15(22) + 36 (2) + 1 = 29
f(3) = 2(33) – 15(32) + 36 (3) + 1 = 28

Thus, we conclude that absolute maximum value of f on [1, 5] is 56, occurring at x =5, and absolute minimum value of f on [1, 5] is 24 which occurs at x = 1.

explanation: Given: {tex}y = \frac{{4\sin \theta }}{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}} - \theta {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{d\theta }} = \frac{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right).4\cos \theta - 4\sin \theta \left( { - \sin \theta } \right)}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}} - 1{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{8\cos \theta + 4{{\cos }^2}\theta + 4{{\sin }^2}\theta }}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}} - 1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{d\theta }} = \frac{{8\cos \theta + 4\left( {{{\cos }^2}\theta + {{\sin }^2}\theta } \right) - {{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{8\cos \theta + 4 - {{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{d\theta }} = \frac{{\left( {8\cos \theta + 4} \right) - \left( {4 + 4\cos \theta + {{\cos }^2}\theta } \right)}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{4\cos \theta - {{\cos }^2}\theta }}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\cos \theta \left( {4 - \cos \theta } \right)}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{/tex} Since {tex}0 \leqslant \theta \leqslant \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} and we have {tex}0 \leqslant \cos \theta \leqslant 1{/tex} , therefore {tex}4 - \cos \theta > 0{/tex} . {tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} \geqslant 0{/tex} for {tex}0 \leqslant \theta \leqslant \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} Hence, y is an increasing function of {tex}\theta {/tex} in {tex}\left[ {0,\frac{\pi }{2}} \right]{/tex}
solution: Given: {tex}y = \frac{{4\sin \theta }}{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}} - \theta {/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{d\theta }} = \frac{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right).4\cos \theta - 4\sin \theta \left( { - \sin \theta } \right)}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}} - 1{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{8\cos \theta + 4{{\cos }^2}\theta + 4{{\sin }^2}\theta }}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}} - 1{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{d\theta }} = \frac{{8\cos \theta + 4\left( {{{\cos }^2}\theta + {{\sin }^2}\theta } \right) - {{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{8\cos \theta + 4 - {{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{d\theta }} = \frac{{\left( {8\cos \theta + 4} \right) - \left( {4 + 4\cos \theta + {{\cos }^2}\theta } \right)}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{4\cos \theta - {{\cos }^2}\theta }}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{\cos \theta \left( {4 - \cos \theta } \right)}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{/tex} Since {tex}0 \leqslant \theta \leqslant \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} and we have {tex}0 \leqslant \cos \theta \leqslant 1{/tex} , therefore {tex}4 - \cos \theta > 0{/tex} . {tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} \geqslant 0{/tex} for {tex}0 \leqslant \theta \leqslant \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} Hence, y is an increasing function of {tex}\theta {/tex} in {tex}\left[ {0,\frac{\pi }{2}} \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: {tex}y = \frac{{4\sin \theta }}{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}} - \theta {/tex} 

{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{d\theta }} = \frac{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right).4\cos \theta - 4\sin \theta \left( { - \sin \theta } \right)}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}} - 1{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{8\cos \theta + 4{{\cos }^2}\theta + 4{{\sin }^2}\theta }}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}} - 1{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{d\theta }} = \frac{{8\cos \theta + 4\left( {{{\cos }^2}\theta + {{\sin }^2}\theta } \right) - {{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{8\cos \theta + 4 - {{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{d\theta }} = \frac{{\left( {8\cos \theta + 4} \right) - \left( {4 + 4\cos \theta + {{\cos }^2}\theta } \right)}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{4\cos \theta - {{\cos }^2}\theta }}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{/tex}

{tex}= \frac{{\cos \theta \left( {4 - \cos \theta } \right)}}{{{{\left( {2 + \cos \theta } \right)}^2}}}{/tex}

Since {tex}0 \leqslant \theta \leqslant \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex} and we have {tex}0 \leqslant \cos \theta \leqslant 1{/tex}, therefore {tex}4 - \cos \theta > 0{/tex}. 

{tex}\therefore \frac{{dy}}{{dx}} \geqslant 0{/tex} for {tex}0 \leqslant \theta \leqslant \frac{\pi }{2}{/tex}

Hence, y is an increasing function of {tex}\theta {/tex} in {tex}\left[ {0,\frac{\pi }{2}} \right]{/tex}

question_html: A ladder 5 m long is leaning against a wall. The bottom of the ladder is pulled along the ground, away from the wall, at the rate of 2cm/s. How fast is its height on the wall decreasing when the foot of the ladder is 4 m away from the wall?
question_text: A ladder 5 m long is leaning against a wall. The bottom of the ladder is pulled along the ground, away from the wall, at the rate of 2cm/s. How fast is its height on the wall decreasing when the foot of the ladder is 4 m away from the wall?
explanation: {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 2cm/s{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 0.02m/\sec {/tex} {tex}{x^2} + {y^2} = {5^2}{/tex} {tex}2x\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} + 2y\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 0{/tex} When x = 4 {tex}y = \sqrt {{5^2} - {4^2}} {/tex} = 3 {tex}2 \times 4\left( {0.02} \right) + 2 \times 3\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 0{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = \frac{{2 \times 4 \times 0.02}}{{2 \times 3}}{/tex} {tex}= - \frac{{0.08}}{3} \times {100}{/tex} {tex} = - \frac{8}{3}cm/\sec {/tex}
solution: {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 2cm/s{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 0.02m/\sec {/tex} {tex}{x^2} + {y^2} = {5^2}{/tex} {tex}2x\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} + 2y\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 0{/tex} When x = 4 {tex}y = \sqrt {{5^2} - {4^2}} {/tex} = 3 {tex}2 \times 4\left( {0.02} \right) + 2 \times 3\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 0{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = \frac{{2 \times 4 \times 0.02}}{{2 \times 3}}{/tex} {tex}= - \frac{{0.08}}{3} \times {100}{/tex} {tex} = - \frac{8}{3}cm/\sec {/tex}
solution_html:
{tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 2cm/s{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} = 0.02m/\sec {/tex} 
{tex}{x^2} + {y^2} = {5^2}{/tex} 
{tex}2x\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} + 2y\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 0{/tex} 
When x = 4
{tex}y = \sqrt {{5^2} - {4^2}} {/tex} = 3
{tex}2 \times 4\left( {0.02} \right) + 2 \times 3\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 0{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = \frac{{2 \times 4 \times 0.02}}{{2 \times 3}}{/tex} 
{tex}= - \frac{{0.08}}{3} \times {100}{/tex} 
{tex} = - \frac{8}{3}cm/\sec {/tex}

question_text: Sand is pouring from a pipe at the rate of 12cm 3 /s. the falling sand forms a cone on the ground in such a way that the height of the cone is always one-sixth of the radius of the base. How fast is the height of the sand cone increasing when the height is 4 cm?
explanation: Let radius of the cone = r Height of the cone = h Volume of the cone {tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi {r^2}h{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = 12c{m^3}/s{/tex} (Given) {tex}h = \frac{1}{6}r{/tex} (Given) {tex}v = \frac{1}{3}\pi {r^2}h{/tex} {tex}v = \frac{1}{3}.\pi .{\left( {6h} \right)^2}.h\,\,\left[ {\because h = \frac{1}{6}r} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}v = \frac{1}{3}.\pi .36.{h^3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow v = 12\pi {h^3}{/tex} Now {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = 12\pi .3{h^2}.\frac{{dh}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}12 = 12\pi .3{\left( 4 \right)^2}.\frac{{dh}}{{dt}}{/tex} [h = 4cm] {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{12}}{{12\pi .3.16}} = \frac{{dh}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{{dh}}{{dt}} = \frac{1}{{48\pi }}cm/s{/tex}
solution: Let radius of the cone = r Height of the cone = h Volume of the cone {tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi {r^2}h{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = 12c{m^3}/s{/tex} (Given) {tex}h = \frac{1}{6}r{/tex} (Given) {tex}v = \frac{1}{3}\pi {r^2}h{/tex} {tex}v = \frac{1}{3}.\pi .{\left( {6h} \right)^2}.h\,\,\left[ {\because h = \frac{1}{6}r} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}v = \frac{1}{3}.\pi .36.{h^3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow v = 12\pi {h^3}{/tex} Now {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = 12\pi .3{h^2}.\frac{{dh}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}12 = 12\pi .3{\left( 4 \right)^2}.\frac{{dh}}{{dt}}{/tex} [h = 4cm] {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{12}}{{12\pi .3.16}} = \frac{{dh}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}\therefore \frac{{dh}}{{dt}} = \frac{1}{{48\pi }}cm/s{/tex}
solution_html:
Let radius of the cone = r
Height of the cone = h
Volume of the cone {tex}= \frac{1}{3}\pi {r^2}h{/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = 12c{m^3}/s{/tex} (Given)
{tex}h = \frac{1}{6}r{/tex} (Given)
{tex}v = \frac{1}{3}\pi {r^2}h{/tex} 
{tex}v = \frac{1}{3}.\pi .{\left( {6h} \right)^2}.h\,\,\left[ {\because h = \frac{1}{6}r} \right]{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}v = \frac{1}{3}.\pi .36.{h^3}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow v = 12\pi {h^3}{/tex} 
Now {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dt}} = 12\pi .3{h^2}.\frac{{dh}}{{dt}}{/tex} 
{tex}12 = 12\pi .3{\left( 4 \right)^2}.\frac{{dh}}{{dt}}{/tex} [h = 4cm]
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{12}}{{12\pi .3.16}} = \frac{{dh}}{{dt}}{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore \frac{{dh}}{{dt}} = \frac{1}{{48\pi }}cm/s{/tex}

question_html: The length x of a rectangle is decreasing at the rate of 5 cm/minute and the width y is increasing at the rate of 4 cm/minute. When x = 8cm and y = 6cm, find the rates of change of (a) the perimeter, and (b) the area of the rectangle.
question_text: The length x of a rectangle is decreasing at the rate of 5 cm/minute and the width y is increasing at the rate of 4 cm/minute. When x = 8cm and y = 6cm, find the rates of change of (a) the perimeter, and (b) the area of the rectangle.
explanation: Given: Rate of decrease of length x of rectangle is 5 cm/minute. {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} is negative = –5 cm/minute {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} = – 5 cm/minute ….(i) Also, Rate of increase of width y of rectangle is 4 cm/minute {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex} is positive= 4 cm/minute {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 4{/tex} cm/minute ….(ii) let z denotes the perimeter of rectangle at any time t {tex}\therefore x = 2x + 2y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dz}}{{dt}} = 2\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} + 2\frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex} (from (i) and (ii) ) {tex}= 2\left( { - 5} \right) + 2\left( 4 \right) = - 2{/tex} which is negative. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Perimeter of the rectangle is decreasing at the rate of 2 cm/min. (b) Let z denotes the area of rectangle at any time t {tex}\Rightarrow z = xy{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dz}}{{dt}} = x\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} + y\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}= 8\left( 4 \right) + 6\left( { - 5} \right) = 2{/tex} which is positive. (from (i) and (ii)) {tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of the rectangle is increasing at the rate of 2 cm 2 /min
solution: Given: Rate of decrease of length x of rectangle is 5 cm/minute. {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} is negative = –5 cm/minute {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} = – 5 cm/minute ….(i) Also, Rate of increase of width y of rectangle is 4 cm/minute {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex} is positive= 4 cm/minute {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 4{/tex} cm/minute ….(ii) let z denotes the perimeter of rectangle at any time t {tex}\therefore x = 2x + 2y{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dz}}{{dt}} = 2\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} + 2\frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex} (from (i) and (ii) ) {tex}= 2\left( { - 5} \right) + 2\left( 4 \right) = - 2{/tex} which is negative. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Perimeter of the rectangle is decreasing at the rate of 2 cm/min. (b) Let z denotes the area of rectangle at any time t {tex}\Rightarrow z = xy{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dz}}{{dt}} = x\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} + y\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} {tex}= 8\left( 4 \right) + 6\left( { - 5} \right) = 2{/tex} which is positive. (from (i) and (ii)) {tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of the rectangle is increasing at the rate of 2 cm 2 /min
solution_html:
Given: Rate of decrease of length x of rectangle is 5 cm/minute.

{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} is negative = –5 cm/minute

{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex} = – 5 cm/minute  ….(i)

Also, Rate of increase of width y of rectangle is 4 cm/minute

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex} is positive= 4 cm/minute

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dy}}{{dt}} = 4{/tex} cm/minute   ….(ii)

let z denotes the perimeter of rectangle at any time t

{tex}\therefore x = 2x + 2y{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{{dz}}{{dt}} = 2\frac{{dx}}{{dt}} + 2\frac{{dy}}{{dt}}{/tex} (from (i) and (ii) )   

{tex}= 2\left( { - 5} \right) + 2\left( 4 \right) = - 2{/tex} which is negative.             

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Perimeter of the rectangle is decreasing at the rate of 2 cm/min.

(b) Let z denotes the area of rectangle at any time t

{tex}\Rightarrow z = xy{/tex}

{tex} \Rightarrow \frac{{dz}}{{dt}} = x\frac{{dy}}{{dt}} + y\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}{/tex}

{tex}= 8\left( 4 \right) + 6\left( { - 5} \right) = 2{/tex} which is positive. (from (i) and (ii))

{tex}\therefore{/tex} Area of the rectangle is increasing at the rate of 2 cm2/min

Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
A gardener wants to construct a rectangular bed of garden in a circular patch of land. He takes the maximum perimeter of the rectangular region as possible. (Refer to the images given below for calculations)

  1. Find the perimeter of rectangle in terms of any one side and radius of circle. (1)
  2. Find critical points to maximize the perimeter of rectangle? (1)
  3. Check for maximum or minimum value of perimeter at critical point. (2)
    OR
    If a rectangle of the maximum perimeter which can be inscribed in a circle of radius 10 cm is square, then the perimeter of region. (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: A gardener wants to construct a rectangular bed of garden in a circular patch of land. He takes the maximum perimeter of the rectangular region as possible. (Refer to the images given below for calculations) Find the perimeter of rectangle in terms of any one side and radius of circle. (1) Find critical points to maximize the perimeter of rectangle? (1) Check for maximum or minimum value of perimeter at critical point. (2) OR If a rectangle of the maximum perimeter which can be inscribed in a circle of radius 10 cm is square, then the perimeter of region. (2)
explanation: Let ‘y’ be the breadth and ‘x’ be the length of rectangle and ‘a’ is radius of given circle. From fig 4a 2 = x 2 + y 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y 2 = 4a 2 - x 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\sqrt {4{a^2}\, - {x^2}}{/tex} Perimeter (P) = 2x + 2y = {tex}2\left( {x + \sqrt {4{a^2}\, - {x^2}} } \right){/tex} We know that P = {tex}2\left(x+\sqrt{4 a^2-x^2}\right){/tex} Critical points to maximize perimeter {tex}\frac{dP}{dx}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d p}{d x}=2\left(1+\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{4 a^2-x^2}}(-2 x)\right){/tex} = 0 {tex}2\left(\frac{\sqrt{4 a^2-x^2}-x}{\sqrt{4 a^2-x^2}}\right){/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{4 a^2-x^2}{/tex} = x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4a 2 - x 2 = x 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2a 2 = x 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\pm \sqrt{2a}{/tex} when x = {tex}\sqrt{2a}{/tex} , y = {tex}\sqrt{2a}{/tex} when x = {tex}-\sqrt{2a}{/tex} not possible as 'x' is length critical point is ( {tex}\sqrt{2a}{/tex} , {tex}\sqrt{2a}{/tex} ) {tex}\frac{d p}{d x}=2\left(1+\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{4 a^2-x^2}}(-2 x)\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{d^2 P}{d x^2}=-2\left(\frac{\sqrt{4 a^2-x^2}-(x)\left(\frac{-2 x}{2 \sqrt{4^2-x^2}}\right)}{\left(4 a^2-x^2\right)}\right){/tex} = {tex}-2\left(\frac{\left(4 a^2-x^2\right)+x^2}{\left(4 a^2-x^2\right)^{3 / 2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\left.\Rightarrow \frac{d^2 P}{d x^2}\right]_{x=a \sqrt{2}}{/tex} = {tex}-2\left(\frac{4 a^2}{\left(4 a^2-2 a^2\right)^{3 / 2}}\right)=\frac{-2}{(2 \sqrt{2}) a}<0{/tex} Perimeter is maximum at a critical point. OR From the above results know that x = y = {tex}\sqrt{2}a{/tex} a = radius Here, x = y = {tex}10\sqrt{2}{/tex} Perimeter = P = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} side = {tex}40\sqrt{2}{/tex} cm
solution: Let ‘y’ be the breadth and ‘x’ be the length of rectangle and ‘a’ is radius of given circle. From fig 4a 2 = x 2 + y 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y 2 = 4a 2 - x 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\sqrt {4{a^2}\, - {x^2}}{/tex} Perimeter (P) = 2x + 2y = {tex}2\left( {x + \sqrt {4{a^2}\, - {x^2}} } \right){/tex} We know that P = {tex}2\left(x+\sqrt{4 a^2-x^2}\right){/tex} Critical points to maximize perimeter {tex}\frac{dP}{dx}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d p}{d x}=2\left(1+\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{4 a^2-x^2}}(-2 x)\right){/tex} = 0 {tex}2\left(\frac{\sqrt{4 a^2-x^2}-x}{\sqrt{4 a^2-x^2}}\right){/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{4 a^2-x^2}{/tex} = x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4a 2 - x 2 = x 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2a 2 = x 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\pm \sqrt{2a}{/tex} when x = {tex}\sqrt{2a}{/tex} , y = {tex}\sqrt{2a}{/tex} when x = {tex}-\sqrt{2a}{/tex} not possible as 'x' is length critical point is ( {tex}\sqrt{2a}{/tex} , {tex}\sqrt{2a}{/tex} ) {tex}\frac{d p}{d x}=2\left(1+\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{4 a^2-x^2}}(-2 x)\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{d^2 P}{d x^2}=-2\left(\frac{\sqrt{4 a^2-x^2}-(x)\left(\frac{-2 x}{2 \sqrt{4^2-x^2}}\right)}{\left(4 a^2-x^2\right)}\right){/tex} = {tex}-2\left(\frac{\left(4 a^2-x^2\right)+x^2}{\left(4 a^2-x^2\right)^{3 / 2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\left.\Rightarrow \frac{d^2 P}{d x^2}\right]_{x=a \sqrt{2}}{/tex} = {tex}-2\left(\frac{4 a^2}{\left(4 a^2-2 a^2\right)^{3 / 2}}\right)=\frac{-2}{(2 \sqrt{2}) a}<0{/tex} Perimeter is maximum at a critical point. OR From the above results know that x = y = {tex}\sqrt{2}a{/tex} a = radius Here, x = y = {tex}10\sqrt{2}{/tex} Perimeter = P = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} side = {tex}40\sqrt{2}{/tex} cm
solution_html:
1. Let ‘y’ be the breadth and ‘x’ be the length of rectangle and ‘a’ is radius of given circle.
    From fig 4a2 = x2 + y2
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y2 = 4a2 - x2
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = {tex}\sqrt {4{a^2}\, - {x^2}}{/tex}
    Perimeter (P) = 2x + 2y = {tex}2\left( {x + \sqrt {4{a^2}\, - {x^2}} } \right){/tex}
  2. We know that P = {tex}2\left(x+\sqrt{4 a^2-x^2}\right){/tex}
    Critical points to maximize perimeter {tex}\frac{dP}{dx}{/tex} = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d p}{d x}=2\left(1+\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{4 a^2-x^2}}(-2 x)\right){/tex} = 0
    {tex}2\left(\frac{\sqrt{4 a^2-x^2}-x}{\sqrt{4 a^2-x^2}}\right){/tex} = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt{4 a^2-x^2}{/tex} = x
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 4a2 - x2 = x2
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2a2 = x2
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\pm \sqrt{2a}{/tex}
    when x = {tex}\sqrt{2a}{/tex}, y = {tex}\sqrt{2a}{/tex}
    when x = {tex}-\sqrt{2a}{/tex} not possible as 'x' is length critical point is ({tex}\sqrt{2a}{/tex}, {tex}\sqrt{2a}{/tex})
  3. {tex}\frac{d p}{d x}=2\left(1+\frac{1}{2 \sqrt{4 a^2-x^2}}(-2 x)\right){/tex}
    {tex}\frac{d^2 P}{d x^2}=-2\left(\frac{\sqrt{4 a^2-x^2}-(x)\left(\frac{-2 x}{2 \sqrt{4^2-x^2}}\right)}{\left(4 a^2-x^2\right)}\right){/tex}
    = {tex}-2\left(\frac{\left(4 a^2-x^2\right)+x^2}{\left(4 a^2-x^2\right)^{3 / 2}}\right){/tex}
    {tex}\left.\Rightarrow \frac{d^2 P}{d x^2}\right]_{x=a \sqrt{2}}{/tex} = {tex}-2\left(\frac{4 a^2}{\left(4 a^2-2 a^2\right)^{3 / 2}}\right)=\frac{-2}{(2 \sqrt{2}) a}<0{/tex} 
    Perimeter is maximum at a critical point.
    OR
    From the above results know that x = y = {tex}\sqrt{2}a{/tex}
    a = radius
    Here, x = y = {tex}10\sqrt{2}{/tex}
    Perimeter = P = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} side = {tex}40\sqrt{2}{/tex} cm

question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
In order to set up a rain water harvesting system, a tank to collect rain water is to be dug. The tank should have a square base and a capacity of 250 m3. The cost of land is ₹ 5,000 per square metre and cost of digging increases with depth and for the whole tank, it is ₹ 40,000 h2, where h is the depth of the tank in metres. x is the side of the square base of the tank in metres.
ELEMENTS OF A TYPICAL RAIN WATER HARVESTING SYSTEM

  1. Find the total cost C of digging the tank in terms of x. (1)
  2. Find {tex}\frac {\text {dC}}{\text {dx}}{/tex}. (1)
  3. Find the value of x for which cost C is minimum. (2)
    OR
    Check whether the cost function C(x) expressed in terms of x is increasing or not, where x > 0. (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: In order to set up a rain water harvesting system, a tank to collect rain water is to be dug. The tank should have a square base and a capacity of 250 m 3 . The cost of land is ₹ 5,000 per square metre and cost of digging increases with depth and for the whole tank, it is ₹ 40,000 h 2 , where h is the depth of the tank in metres. x is the side of the square base of the tank in metres. ELEMENTS OF A TYPICAL RAIN WATER HARVESTING SYSTEM Find the total cost C of digging the tank in terms of x. (1) Find {tex}\frac {\text {dC}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} . (1) Find the value of x for which cost C is minimum. (2) OR Check whether the cost function C(x) expressed in terms of x is increasing or not, where x > 0. (2)
explanation: C = 40000h 2 + 5000x 2 as x 2 h = 250 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = {tex}\frac{40000(250)^2}{\text x^4}{/tex} + 5000x 2 {tex}\frac {\text {dC}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-160000(250)^2}{\text x^5}{/tex} + 10000x For minimum cost {tex}\frac {dc}{dx}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 10000x 6 = 250 {tex}\times{/tex} 250 {tex}\times{/tex} 160000 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 10 showing {tex}\frac{\text d^2 \text C}{\text {dx}^2}{/tex} > 0 at x = 10 {tex}\therefore{/tex} cost is minimum when x = 10 OR {tex}\frac{\text {dC}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-160000(250)^2}{\text x^4}{/tex} + 10000x {tex}\frac{\text {dC}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = 0 gives x = 10 {tex}\frac{\text {dC}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} > 0 in (10, {tex}\infty{/tex} ) and {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dC}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} < 0 in (0, 10). Hence, cost function is neither increasing nor decreasing for x > 0
solution: C = 40000h 2 + 5000x 2 as x 2 h = 250 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = {tex}\frac{40000(250)^2}{\text x^4}{/tex} + 5000x 2 {tex}\frac {\text {dC}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-160000(250)^2}{\text x^5}{/tex} + 10000x For minimum cost {tex}\frac {dc}{dx}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 10000x 6 = 250 {tex}\times{/tex} 250 {tex}\times{/tex} 160000 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 10 showing {tex}\frac{\text d^2 \text C}{\text {dx}^2}{/tex} > 0 at x = 10 {tex}\therefore{/tex} cost is minimum when x = 10 OR {tex}\frac{\text {dC}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-160000(250)^2}{\text x^4}{/tex} + 10000x {tex}\frac{\text {dC}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = 0 gives x = 10 {tex}\frac{\text {dC}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} > 0 in (10, {tex}\infty{/tex} ) and {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dC}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} < 0 in (0, 10). Hence, cost function is neither increasing nor decreasing for x > 0
solution_html:
1. C = 40000h2 + 5000x2
    as x2h = 250
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = {tex}\frac{40000(250)^2}{\text x^4}{/tex} + 5000x2

  2. {tex}\frac {\text {dC}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-160000(250)^2}{\text x^5}{/tex} + 10000x

  3. For minimum cost {tex}\frac {dc}{dx}{/tex} = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 10000x6 = 250 {tex}\times{/tex} 250 {tex}\times{/tex} 160000
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 10
    showing {tex}\frac{\text d^2 \text C}{\text {dx}^2}{/tex} > 0 at x = 10
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} cost is minimum when x = 10
    OR
    {tex}\frac{\text {dC}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-160000(250)^2}{\text x^4}{/tex} + 10000x
    {tex}\frac{\text {dC}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} = 0 gives x = 10
    {tex}\frac{\text {dC}}{\text {dx}}{/tex} > 0 in (10, {tex}\infty{/tex}) and {tex}\frac{\mathrm{dC}}{\mathrm{dx}}{/tex} < 0 in (0, 10).
    Hence, cost function is neither increasing nor decreasing for x > 0

question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
The relation between the height of the plant (y in cm) with respect to exposure to sunlight is governed by the following equation y = 4x - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}x2 where x is the number of days exposed to sunlight.

  1. Find the rate of growth of the plant with respect to sunlight. (1)
  2. What is the number of days it will take for the plant to grow to the maximum height? (1)
  3. Verify that height of the plant is maximum after four days by second derivative test and find the maximum height of plant. (2)
    OR
    What will be the height of the plant after 2 days? (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: The relation between the height of the plant (y in cm) with respect to exposure to sunlight is governed by the following equation y = 4x - {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} x 2 where x is the number of days exposed to sunlight. Find the rate of growth of the plant with respect to sunlight. (1) What is the number of days it will take for the plant to grow to the maximum height? (1) Verify that height of the plant is maximum after four days by second derivative test and find the maximum height of plant. (2) OR What will be the height of the plant after 2 days? (2)
explanation: The rate of growth = {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{d(4x-\frac{1}{2}x^2)}{dx}{/tex} = 4 - x For the height to be maximum or minimum {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d\left(4 x-\frac{1}{2} x^2\right)}{d x}=4-\frac{1}{2} \cdot 2 x{/tex} = 0 {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = 4 - x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 4 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Number of required days = 4 {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = 4 - x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}{/tex} = -1 < 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Function attains maximum value at x = 4 We have y = 4x - {tex}\frac{1}{2} x^2{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} when x = 4 the height of the plant will be maximum which is y = {tex}4\times 4- \frac{1}{2}\times (4)^2{/tex} = 16 - 8 = 8 cm OR We have, y = 4x - {tex}\frac{1}{2}x^2{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} When x = 4 the height of the plant will be maximum which is y = {tex}4 \times 4 -\frac{1}{2} \times (4)^2{/tex} = 8 - 2 = 6 cm
solution: The rate of growth = {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{d(4x-\frac{1}{2}x^2)}{dx}{/tex} = 4 - x For the height to be maximum or minimum {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d\left(4 x-\frac{1}{2} x^2\right)}{d x}=4-\frac{1}{2} \cdot 2 x{/tex} = 0 {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = 4 - x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 4 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Number of required days = 4 {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = 4 - x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}{/tex} = -1 < 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Function attains maximum value at x = 4 We have y = 4x - {tex}\frac{1}{2} x^2{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} when x = 4 the height of the plant will be maximum which is y = {tex}4\times 4- \frac{1}{2}\times (4)^2{/tex} = 16 - 8 = 8 cm OR We have, y = 4x - {tex}\frac{1}{2}x^2{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} When x = 4 the height of the plant will be maximum which is y = {tex}4 \times 4 -\frac{1}{2} \times (4)^2{/tex} = 8 - 2 = 6 cm
solution_html:
1. The rate of growth = {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} 
    = {tex}\frac{d(4x-\frac{1}{2}x^2)}{dx}{/tex} 
    = 4 - x
  2. For the height to be maximum or minimum
    {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d\left(4 x-\frac{1}{2} x^2\right)}{d x}=4-\frac{1}{2} \cdot 2 x{/tex} = 0
    {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = 4 - x = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 4
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Number of required days = 4
  3. {tex}\frac{dy}{dx}{/tex} = 4 - x
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^2y}{dx^2}{/tex} = -1 < 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Function attains maximum value at x = 4
    We have
    y = 4x - {tex}\frac{1}{2} x^2{/tex}
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} when x = 4 the height of the plant will be maximum which is y = {tex}4\times 4- \frac{1}{2}\times (4)^2{/tex} = 16 - 8 = 8 cm
    OR

    y = {tex}4 \times 4 -\frac{1}{2} \times (4)^2{/tex}
    = 8 - 2 = 6 cm

Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
An Apache helicopter of the enemy is flying along the curve given by y = x2 + 7. A soldier, placed at (3, 7) want to shoot down the helicopter when it is nearest to him.

  1. If P (x1, y1) be the position of a helicopter on curve y = x2 + 7, then find distance D from P to soldier place at (3, 7). (1)
  2. Find the critical point such that distance is minimum. (1)
  3. Verify by second derivative test that distance is minimum at (1, 8). (2)
    OR
    Find the minimum distance between soldier and helicopter? (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: An Apache helicopter of the enemy is flying along the curve given by y = x 2 + 7. A soldier, placed at (3, 7) want to shoot down the helicopter when it is nearest to him. If P (x 1 , y 1 ) be the position of a helicopter on curve y = x 2 + 7, then find distance D from P to soldier place at (3, 7). (1) Find the critical point such that distance is minimum. (1) Verify by second derivative test that distance is minimum at (1, 8). (2) OR Find the minimum distance between soldier and helicopter? (2)
explanation: P(x 1 , y 1 ) is on the curve y = x 2 + 7 {tex}\Rightarrow {y_1} = \,x_1^2 + 7{/tex} Distance from p {tex}({x_1},x_1^2 + 7){/tex} and (3, 7) D = {tex}\sqrt {{{\left( {{x_1} - 3} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {x_1^2 + 7 - 7} \right)}^2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt {{{\left( {{x_1} - 3} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {x_1^2} \right)}^2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow D = \sqrt {x_1^4 + x_1^2 - 6{x_1} + 9}{/tex} D = {tex}\sqrt {x_1^4 + x_1^2 - 6{x_1} + 9}{/tex} D' = {tex}x_1^4 + x_1^2 - 6{x_1} + 9{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dD'}}{{dx}} = 4x_1^3 + 2{x_1} - 6 = 0{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dD'}}{{dx}} = 2x_1^3 + {x_1} - 3 = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x 1 - 1) {tex}\left( {2{x_1}^2 + 2{x_1} + 3} \right){/tex} = 0 x 1 = 1 and {tex}2x_1^2{/tex} + 2x 1 + 3 = 0 gives no real roots The critical point is (1, 8). {tex}\frac{{dD'}}{{dx}} = 4x_1^3 + 2{x_1} - 6{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{d^2}D'}}{{d{x^2}}} = 12x_1^2 + 2{/tex} {tex}{\left. {\frac{{{d^2}D'}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right]_{{x_1} = 1}}{/tex} = 12 + 2 = 14 > 0 Hence distance is minimum at (1, 8). OR {tex}D = \sqrt {x_1^4 + x_1^2 - 6{x_1} + 9}{/tex} {tex}D = \sqrt {1 + 1 - 6 + 9} = \sqrt 5{/tex} units
solution: P(x 1 , y 1 ) is on the curve y = x 2 + 7 {tex}\Rightarrow {y_1} = \,x_1^2 + 7{/tex} Distance from p {tex}({x_1},x_1^2 + 7){/tex} and (3, 7) D = {tex}\sqrt {{{\left( {{x_1} - 3} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {x_1^2 + 7 - 7} \right)}^2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt {{{\left( {{x_1} - 3} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {x_1^2} \right)}^2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow D = \sqrt {x_1^4 + x_1^2 - 6{x_1} + 9}{/tex} D = {tex}\sqrt {x_1^4 + x_1^2 - 6{x_1} + 9}{/tex} D' = {tex}x_1^4 + x_1^2 - 6{x_1} + 9{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dD'}}{{dx}} = 4x_1^3 + 2{x_1} - 6 = 0{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dD'}}{{dx}} = 2x_1^3 + {x_1} - 3 = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x 1 - 1) {tex}\left( {2{x_1}^2 + 2{x_1} + 3} \right){/tex} = 0 x 1 = 1 and {tex}2x_1^2{/tex} + 2x 1 + 3 = 0 gives no real roots The critical point is (1, 8). {tex}\frac{{dD'}}{{dx}} = 4x_1^3 + 2{x_1} - 6{/tex} {tex}\frac{{{d^2}D'}}{{d{x^2}}} = 12x_1^2 + 2{/tex} {tex}{\left. {\frac{{{d^2}D'}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right]_{{x_1} = 1}}{/tex} = 12 + 2 = 14 > 0 Hence distance is minimum at (1, 8). OR {tex}D = \sqrt {x_1^4 + x_1^2 - 6{x_1} + 9}{/tex} {tex}D = \sqrt {1 + 1 - 6 + 9} = \sqrt 5{/tex} units
solution_html:
1. P(x1, y1) is on the curve y = x2 + 7 {tex}\Rightarrow {y_1} = \,x_1^2 + 7{/tex}
    Distance from p{tex}({x_1},x_1^2 + 7){/tex} and (3, 7)
    D = {tex}\sqrt {{{\left( {{x_1} - 3} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {x_1^2 + 7 - 7} \right)}^2}}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \sqrt {{{\left( {{x_1} - 3} \right)}^2} + {{\left( {x_1^2} \right)}^2}}{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow D = \sqrt {x_1^4 + x_1^2 - 6{x_1} + 9}{/tex}
     
  2. D = {tex}\sqrt {x_1^4 + x_1^2 - 6{x_1} + 9}{/tex}
    D' = {tex}x_1^4 + x_1^2 - 6{x_1} + 9{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{{dD'}}{{dx}} = 4x_1^3 + 2{x_1} - 6 = 0{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{{dD'}}{{dx}} = 2x_1^3 + {x_1} - 3 = 0{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x1 - 1){tex}\left( {2{x_1}^2 + 2{x_1} + 3} \right){/tex}= 0
    x1 = 1 and {tex}2x_1^2{/tex} + 2x1 + 3 = 0 gives no real roots
    The critical point is (1, 8).
  3. {tex}\frac{{dD'}}{{dx}} = 4x_1^3 + 2{x_1} - 6{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{{{d^2}D'}}{{d{x^2}}} = 12x_1^2 + 2{/tex}
    {tex}{\left. {\frac{{{d^2}D'}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right]_{{x_1} = 1}}{/tex} = 12 + 2 = 14 > 0
    Hence distance is minimum at (1, 8).
    OR
    {tex}D = \sqrt {x_1^4 + x_1^2 - 6{x_1} + 9}{/tex}
    {tex}D = \sqrt {1 + 1 - 6 + 9} = \sqrt 5{/tex} units

Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
Naina is creative she wants to prepare a sweet box for Diwali at home. She took a square piece of cardboard of side 18 cm which is to be made into an open box, by cutting a square from each corner and folding up the flaps to form the box. She wants to cover the top of the box with some decorative paper. Naina is interested in maximizing the volume of the box.

  1. Find the volume of the open box formed by folding up the cutting each corner with x cm. (1)
  2. Naina is interested in maximizing the volume of the box. So, what should be the side of the square to be cut off so that the volume of the box is maximum? (1)
  3. Verify that volume of the box is maximum at x = 3 cm by second derivative test? (2)
    OR
    Find the maximum volume of the box. (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: Naina is creative she wants to prepare a sweet box for Diwali at home. She took a square piece of cardboard of side 18 cm which is to be made into an open box, by cutting a square from each corner and folding up the flaps to form the box. She wants to cover the top of the box with some decorative paper. Naina is interested in maximizing the volume of the box. Find the volume of the open box formed by folding up the cutting each corner with x cm. (1) Naina is interested in maximizing the volume of the box. So, what should be the side of the square to be cut off so that the volume of the box is maximum? (1) Verify that volume of the box is maximum at x = 3 cm by second derivative test? (2) OR Find the maximum volume of the box. (2)
explanation: Let the side of square to be cut off be 'x' cm. then, the length and the breadth of the box will be (18 - 2x) cm each and the height of the box is 'x' cm. The volume V(x) of the box is given by V(x) = x(18 - x) 2 V(x) = x(18 - 2x) 2 {tex}\frac{dV(x)}{dx}{/tex} = (18 - 2x) 2 - 4x(18 - 2x) For maxima or minima = {tex}\frac{dV(x)}{dx}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (18 - 2x)[18 - 2x - 4x] = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 9 or x = 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = not possible {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 3 cm The side of the square to be cut off so that the volume of the box is maximum is x = 3 cm {tex}\frac{d V(x)}{d x}{/tex} = (18 - 2x)(18 - 6x) {tex}\frac{d^2 V(x)}{d x^2}{/tex} = (18 - 6x)(-2) + (18 - 2x)(-6) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^2 V(x)}{d x^2}{/tex} = -12[3 - x + 9 - x] = -24(6 - x) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}{\left. {\frac{{{d^2}V(x)}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right]_{x = 3}}{/tex} = -72 < 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} volume is maximum at x = 3 OR V(x) = x(18 - 2x) 2 When x = 3 V(3) = 3(18 - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3) 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Volume = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 = 432 cm 3
solution: Let the side of square to be cut off be 'x' cm. then, the length and the breadth of the box will be (18 - 2x) cm each and the height of the box is 'x' cm. The volume V(x) of the box is given by V(x) = x(18 - x) 2 V(x) = x(18 - 2x) 2 {tex}\frac{dV(x)}{dx}{/tex} = (18 - 2x) 2 - 4x(18 - 2x) For maxima or minima = {tex}\frac{dV(x)}{dx}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (18 - 2x)[18 - 2x - 4x] = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 9 or x = 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = not possible {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 3 cm The side of the square to be cut off so that the volume of the box is maximum is x = 3 cm {tex}\frac{d V(x)}{d x}{/tex} = (18 - 2x)(18 - 6x) {tex}\frac{d^2 V(x)}{d x^2}{/tex} = (18 - 6x)(-2) + (18 - 2x)(-6) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^2 V(x)}{d x^2}{/tex} = -12[3 - x + 9 - x] = -24(6 - x) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}{\left. {\frac{{{d^2}V(x)}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right]_{x = 3}}{/tex} = -72 < 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} volume is maximum at x = 3 OR V(x) = x(18 - 2x) 2 When x = 3 V(3) = 3(18 - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3) 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Volume = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 = 432 cm 3
solution_html:
1. Let the side of square to be cut off be 'x' cm. then, the length and the breadth of the box will be (18 - 2x) cm each and the height of the box is 'x' cm.
    The volume V(x) of the box is given by V(x) = x(18 - x)2
  2. V(x) = x(18 - 2x)2
    {tex}\frac{dV(x)}{dx}{/tex} = (18 - 2x)2 - 4x(18 - 2x)
    For maxima or minima = {tex}\frac{dV(x)}{dx}{/tex} = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (18 - 2x)[18 - 2x - 4x] = 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 9 or x = 3
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = not possible
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 3 cm
    The side of the square to be cut off so that the volume of the box is maximum is x = 3 cm
  3. {tex}\frac{d V(x)}{d x}{/tex} = (18 - 2x)(18 - 6x)
    {tex}\frac{d^2 V(x)}{d x^2}{/tex} = (18 - 6x)(-2) + (18 - 2x)(-6)
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{d^2 V(x)}{d x^2}{/tex} = -12[3 - x + 9 - x] = -24(6 - x)
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}{\left. {\frac{{{d^2}V(x)}}{{d{x^2}}}} \right]_{x = 3}}{/tex} = -72 < 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} volume is maximum at x = 3
    OR
    V(x) = x(18 - 2x)2
    When x = 3
    V(3) = 3(18 - 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 3)2
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Volume = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 {tex}\times{/tex} 12 = 432 cm3

question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
On the request of villagers, a construction agency designs a tank with the help of an architect. Tank consists of a rectangular base with rectangular sides, open at the top so that its depth is 2 m and volume is 8 m3 as shown below. The construction of the tank costs ₹70 per sq. metre for the base and ₹45 per square metre for sides.

  1. Express making cost C in terms of length of rectangle base. (1)
  2. If x and y represent the length and breadth of its rectangular base, then find the relation between the variables. (1)
  3. Find the value of x so that the cost of construction is minimum. (2)
    OR
    Verify by second derivative test that cost is minimum at a critical point. (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: On the request of villagers, a construction agency designs a tank with the help of an architect. Tank consists of a rectangular base with rectangular sides, open at the top so that its depth is 2 m and volume is 8 m 3 as shown below. The construction of the tank costs ₹70 per sq. metre for the base and ₹45 per square metre for sides. Express making cost C in terms of length of rectangle base. (1) If x and y represent the length and breadth of its rectangular base, then find the relation between the variables. (1) Find the value of x so that the cost of construction is minimum. (2) OR Verify by second derivative test that cost is minimum at a critical point. (2)
explanation: Since 'C' is cost of making tank {tex}\therefore{/tex} C = 70xy + 45 {tex}\times{/tex} 2(2x + 2y) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 70xy + 90(2x + 2y) {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 70xy + 180(x + y) [ {tex}\because{/tex} 2 {tex}\cdot x\cdot y{/tex} = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow y = \frac 8{2x} \Rightarrow y = \frac 4x{/tex} ] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 70x {tex}\times \frac{4}{x}+180\left(x+\frac{4}{x}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = 280 + 180 {tex}\left(x+\frac{4}{x}\right){/tex} x {tex}\cdot{/tex} y = 4 Volume of tank = length {tex}\times{/tex} breadth {tex}\times{/tex} height (Depth) 8 = {tex}x\cdot y \cdot{/tex} 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2xy = 8 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} xy = 4 For maximum or minimum {tex}\frac{d C}{d x}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\frac{d}{d x}{/tex} (280 + 180(x + {tex}\frac{4}{x}{/tex} )) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 180 {tex}\left(1+4\left(-\frac{1}{x^{2}}\right)\right){/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 180 {tex}\left(1-\frac{4}{x^{2}}\right){/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow 1-\frac{4}{x^{2}}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{4}{x^{2}}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\pm{/tex} 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2 (length can never be negative) OR Now, {tex}\frac{d^{2} C}{d x^{2}}{/tex} = 180 {tex}\left(+\frac{8}{x^{3}}\right){/tex} {tex}\left.\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} C}{d x^{2}}\right|_{x=2}{/tex} = 180 {tex}\times \frac{8}{8}{/tex} = 180 = +ve Hence, to minimize C, x = 2m

    {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{4}{x^{2}}{/tex} = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 = 4
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\pm{/tex}2
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2 (length can never be negative)
    OR

    {tex}\left.\Rightarrow \frac{d^{2} C}{d x^{2}}\right|_{x=2}{/tex} = 180 {tex}\times \frac{8}{8}{/tex} = 180 = +ve
    Hence, to minimize C, x = 2m

A tin can manufacturer designs a cylindrical tin can for a company making sanitizer and disinfectors. The tin can is made to hold 3 litres of sanitizer or disinfector. The cost of material used to manufacture the tin can is ₹100/m2.

  1. If r cm be the radius and h cm be the height of the cylindrical tin can, then express the surface area as a function of radius (r)? (1)
  2. Find the radius of the can that will minimize the cost of tin used for making can? (1)
  3. Find the height that will minimize the cost of tin used for making can? (2)
    OR

question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: A tin can manufacturer designs a cylindrical tin can for a company making sanitizer and disinfectors. (1) Find the radius of the can that will minimize the cost of tin used for making can? (1) Find the height that will minimize the cost of tin used for making can? (2) OR Find the minimum cost of material used to manufacture the tin can. (2)
explanation: Given, r cm is the radius and h cm is the height of required cylindrical can. Given that, volume of cylinder = 3l = 3000 cm 3 ( {tex}\because{/tex} 1l = 1000 cm 3 ) {tex}\Rightarrow \pi r^{2} h{/tex} = 3000 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = {tex}\frac{3000}{\pi r^{2}}{/tex} Now, the surface area, as a function of r is given by S(r) = {tex}2 \pi r^{2}+2 \pi r h=2 \pi r^{2}+2 \pi r\left(\frac{3000}{\pi r^{2}}\right){/tex} = 2 {tex} \pi {/tex} r 2 + {tex}\frac{6000}{r}{/tex} Now, S(r) = {tex}2 \pi r^{2}+\frac{6000}{r}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} S'(r) = {tex}4 \pi r-\frac{6000}{r^{2}}{/tex} To find critical points, put S'(r) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{4 \pi r^{3}-6000}{r^{2}}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} r 3 = {tex}\frac{6000}{4 \pi} \Rightarrow r=\left(\frac{1500}{\pi}\right)^{1 / 3}{/tex} Also, {tex}\left.S^{\prime \prime}(r)\right|_{r}=\sqrt[3]{\frac{1500}{\pi}}=4 \pi+\frac{12000 \times \pi}{1500}{/tex} = {tex}4 \pi + 8 \pi{/tex} = {tex}12\pi{/tex} > 0 Thus, the critical point is the point of minima. The cost of material for the tin can is minimized when r = {tex}\sqrt[3]{\frac{1500}{\pi}}{/tex} cm and the height is {tex}\frac{3000}{\pi\left(\sqrt[3]{\frac{1500}{\pi}}\right)^{2}}=2 \sqrt[3]{\frac{1500}{\pi}}{/tex} cm. OR We have, minimum surface area = {tex}\frac{2 \pi r^{3}+6000}{r}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{2 \pi \cdot \frac{1500}{\pi}+6000}{\sqrt[3]{\frac{1500}{\pi}}}=\frac{9000}{7.8}{/tex} = 1153.84 cm 2 Cost of 1 m 2 material = ₹100 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Cost of 1 cm 2 material = {tex}\text { ₹ } \frac{1}{100}{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Minimum cost = ₹ {tex} \frac{1153.84}{100}{/tex} = ₹11.538

Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
In an elliptical sport field, the authority wants to design a rectangular soccer field with the maximum possible area. The sport field is given by the graph of {tex}\frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}{/tex} = I
.

  1. If the length and the breadth of the rectangular field be 2x and 2y respectively, then find the area function in terms of x. (1)
  2. Find the critical point of the function. (1)
  3. Use First Derivative Test to find the lenght 2x and width 2y of the soccer field (in terms of a and b) that maximize its area. (2)
    OR
  4. Use Second Derivative Test to find the length 2x and width 2y of the soccer field (in terms of a and b) that maximize its area. (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: In an elliptical sport field, the authority wants to design a rectangular soccer field with the maximum possible area. The sport field is given by the graph of {tex}\frac{x^2}{a^2}+\frac{y^2}{b^2}{/tex} = I . If the length and the breadth of the rectangular field be 2x and 2y respectively, then find the area function in terms of x. (1) Find the critical point of the function. (1) Use First Derivative Test to find the lenght 2x and width 2y of the soccer field (in terms of a and b) that maximize its area. (2) OR Use Second Derivative Test to find the length 2x and width 2y of the soccer field (in terms of a and b) that maximize its area. (2)
explanation: Let (x, y) = {tex}\left(x, \frac{b}{a} \sqrt{a^2-x^2}\right){/tex} be the upper right vertex of the rectangle. The area function A = 2x {tex}\times {/tex} {tex}2\frac ba \sqrt{a^2-x^2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4 b}{a} x \sqrt{a^2-x^2}{/tex} , x {tex}\in{/tex} (0, a). {tex}\frac{d A}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4 b}{a}\left[x \times \frac{-x}{\sqrt{a^2-x^2}}+\sqrt{a^2-x^2}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{4 b}{a} \times \frac{a^2-2 x^2}{\sqrt{a^2-x^2}}{/tex} = {tex}-\frac{4 b}{a} \times \frac{2\left(x+\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\left(x-\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}\right)}{\sqrt{a^2-x^2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d A}{d x}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} x = {tex}\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} . x = {tex}\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}} {/tex} is the critical point. For the values of x less than {tex}\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} and close to {tex}\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} , {tex}\frac{d A}{d x}{/tex} > 0 and for the values of x greater than {tex}\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} and close to {tex}\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} , {tex}\frac{d A}{d x}{/tex} < 0. Hence, by the first derivative test, there is a local maximum at the critical point x = {tex}\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} . Since, there is only one critical point, therefore, the area of the soccer field is maximum at this critical point x = {tex}\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} . Thus, for maximum area of the soccer field, its length should be {tex}a \sqrt{2}{/tex} and its width should be {tex}b \sqrt{2}{/tex} . OR A = 2x {tex}\times {/tex} {tex}2 \frac{b}{a} \sqrt{a^2-x^2}{/tex} , x {tex}\in{/tex} (0, a). Squaring both sides, we get Z = A 2 = {tex}\frac{16 b^2}{a^2}{/tex} x 2 (a 2 - x 2 ) = {tex}\frac{16 b^2}{a^2}{/tex} (x 2 a 2 - x 4 ), x {tex}\in{/tex} (0, a) A is maximum when Z is maximum. {tex}\frac{d Z}{d x}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{16 b^2}{a^2}\left(2 x a^2-4 x^3\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{32 b^2}{a^2} x(a+\sqrt{2 x}){/tex} (a - {tex}\sqrt{2 x}{/tex} ) {tex}\frac{d Z}{d x}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} x = {tex}\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} . {tex}\frac{d^2 Z}{d x^2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{32 b^2}{a^2}\left(a^2-6 x^2\right){/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{d^2 Z}{d x^2}\right)_{x=\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}} =\frac{32 b^2}{a^2}{/tex} (a 2 - 3a 2 ) = -64b 2 < 0 Hence, by the second derivative test, there is a local maximum value of Z at the critical point x = {tex}\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} . Since there is only one critical point, therefore, Z is maximum at x = {tex}\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} , hence, A is maximum at x = {tex}\frac{a}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} . Thus, for maximum area of the soccer field, its length should be {tex}a{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} and its width should be {tex}b{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} .

Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
Mrs. Maya is the owner of a high-rise residential society having 50 apartments. When he set rent at ₹10000/month, all apartments are rented. If he increases rent by ₹250/ month, one fewer apartment is rented. The maintenance cost for each occupied unit is ₹500/month.

  1. If P is the rent price per apartment and N is the number of rented apartments, then find the profit. (1)
  2. If x represents the number of apartments which are not rented, then express profit as a function of x. (1)
  3. Find the number of apartments which are not rented so that profit is maximum. (2)
    OR
    Verify that profit is maximum at critical value of x by second derivative test. (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: Mrs. Maya is the owner of a high-rise residential society having 50 apartments. When he set rent at ₹10000/month, all apartments are rented. If he increases rent by ₹250/ month, one fewer apartment is rented. The maintenance cost for each occupied unit is ₹500/month. If P is the rent price per apartment and N is the number of rented apartments, then find the profit. (1) If x represents the number of apartments which are not rented, then express profit as a function of x. (1) Find the number of apartments which are not rented so that profit is maximum. (2) OR Verify that profit is maximum at critical value of x by second derivative test. (2)
explanation: If P is the rent price per apartment and N is the number of rented apartments, the profit is given by NP - 500 N = N(P - 500) [ {tex}\because{/tex} ₹500/month is the maintenance charge for each occupied unit] Let R be the rent price per apartment and N is the number of rented apartments. Now, if x be the number of non-rented apartments, then N(x) = 50 - x and R(x) = 10000 + 250x Thus, profit = P(x) = NR = (50 - x) (10000 + 250 x - 500) = (50 - x) (9500 + 250 x) = 250(50 - x) (38 + x) We have, P(x) = 250(50 - x) (38 + x) Now, P'(x) = 250[50 - x - (38 + x)] = 250[12 - 2x] For maxima/minima, put P'(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 12 - 2x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 6 Number of apartments are 6. OR P′(x) = 250(12 - 2x) P′′(x) = - 500 < 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} P(x) is maximum at x = 6

question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
In a street two lamp posts are 600 feet apart. The light intensity at a distance d from the first (stronger) lamp post is {tex}\frac{1000}{d^2}{/tex}, the light intensity at distance d from the second (weaker) lamp post is {tex}\frac{125}{d^2}{/tex} (in both cases the light intensity is inversely proportional to the square of the distance to the light source). The combined light intensity is the sum of the two light intensities coming from both lamp posts.

  1. If l(x) denotes the combined light intensity, then find the value of x so that I(x) is minimum. (1)
  2. Find the darkest spot between the two lights. (1)
  3. If you are in between the lamp posts, at distance x feet from the stronger light, then write the combined light intensity coming from both lamp posts as function of x. (2)
    OR
    Find the minimum combined light intensity? (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: In a street two lamp posts are 600 feet apart. The light intensity at a distance d from the first (stronger) lamp post is {tex}\frac{1000}{d^2}{/tex} , the light intensity at distance d from the second (weaker) lamp post is {tex}\frac{125}{d^2}{/tex} (in both cases the light intensity is inversely proportional to the square of the distance to the light source). The combined light intensity is the sum of the two light intensities coming from both lamp posts. If l(x) denotes the combined light intensity, then find the value of x so that I(x) is minimum. (1) Find the darkest spot between the two lights. (1) If you are in between the lamp posts, at distance x feet from the stronger light, then write the combined light intensity coming from both lamp posts as function of x. (2) OR Find the minimum combined light intensity? (2)
explanation: We have, I(x) = {tex}\frac{1000}{x^{2}}+\frac{125}{(600-x)^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I'(x) = {tex}\frac{-2000}{x^{3}}+\frac{250}{(600-x)^{3}}{/tex} and {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} I''(x) = {tex}\frac{6000}{x^{4}}+\frac{750}{(600-x)^{4}}{/tex} For maxima/minima, I'(x) - 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{2000}{x^{3}}=\frac{250}{(600-x)^{3}}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 8(600 - x) 3 = x 3 Taking cube root on both sides, we get 2(600 - x) = x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1200 = 3x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 400 Thus, I(x) is minimum when you are at 400 feet from the strong intensity lamp post. At a distance of 200 feet from the weaker lamp post. Since I(x) is minimum when x = 400 feet, therefore the darkest spot between the two light is at a distance of 400 feet from a stronger lamp post, i.e., at a distance of 600 - 400 = 200 feet from the weaker lamp post. {tex}\frac{1000}{x^{2}}+\frac{125}{(600-x)^{2}}{/tex} Since, the distance is x feet from the stronger light, therefore the distance from the weaker light will be 600 - x. So, the combined light intensity from both lamp posts is given by {tex}\frac{1000}{x^{2}}+\frac{125}{(600-x)^{2}}{/tex} . OR We know that l(x) = {tex}\frac{1000}{x^2}+\frac{125}{(600-x)^2}{/tex} When x = 400 l(x) = {tex}\frac{1000}{160000}+\frac{125}{(600-400)^2}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{160}+\frac{125}{40000}=\frac{1}{160}+\frac{1}{320}=\frac{3}{320}{/tex} units

question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
The temperature of a person during an intestinal illness is given by f(x) = -0.1x2 + mx + 98.6, 0 {tex}\leq{/tex} x < 12, m being a constant, where f(x) is the temperature in oF at x days.

  1. Is the function differentiable in the interval (0, 12)? Justify your answer. (1)
  2. If 6 is the critical point of the function, then find the value of the constant m. (1)
  3. Find the intervals in which the function is strictly increasing/strictly decreasing. (2)
    OR
    Find the points of local maximum/local minimum, if any, in the interval (0, 12) as well as the points of absolute maximum/absolute minimum in the interval [0, 12]. Also, find the corresponding local minimum and the absolute maximum/absolute minimum values of the function. (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: The temperature of a person during an intestinal illness is given by f(x) = -0.1x 2 + mx + 98.6, 0 {tex}\leq{/tex} x < 12, m being a constant, where f(x) is the temperature in o F at x days. Is the function differentiable in the interval (0, 12)? Justify your answer. (1) If 6 is the critical point of the function, then find the value of the constant m. (1) Find the intervals in which the function is strictly increasing/strictly decreasing. (2) OR Find the points of local maximum/local minimum, if any, in the interval (0, 12) as well as the points of absolute maximum/absolute minimum in the interval [0, 12]. Also, find the corresponding local minimum and the absolute maximum/absolute minimum values of the function. (2)
explanation: f(x) = -0.1x 2 + mx + 98.6, being a polynomial function, is differentiable everywhere, hence, differentiable in (0, 12). f(x) = -0.2x + m At Critical point 0 = -0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 6 + m m = 1.2 f(x) = -0.1x 2 + 1.2x + 98.6 f'(x) = -0.2x + 1.2 = -0.2(x - 6) In the Interval f'(x) Conclusion (0, 6) +Ve f is strictly increasing in [0, 6] (6, 12) -Ve f is strictly decreasing in [6, 12] OR f(x) = -0.1x 2 + 1.2x + 98.6, f'(x) = -0.2x + 1.2, f'(6) = 0, f"(x) = -0.2 f"(6) = -0.2 < 0 Hence, by second derivative test 6 is a point of local maximum. The local maximum value = f(6) = -0.1 {tex}\times {/tex} 6 2 + 1.2 {tex}\times {/tex} 6 + 98.6 = 102.2 We have f(0) = 98.6, f(6) = 102.2, f(12) = 98.6 6 is the point of absolute maximum and the absolute maximum value of the function = 102.2. 0 and 12 both are the points of absolute minimum and the absolute minimum value of the function = 98.6.
solution: f(x) = -0.1x 2 + mx + 98.6, being a polynomial function, is differentiable everywhere, hence, differentiable in (0, 12). f(x) = -0.2x + m At Critical point 0 = -0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 6 + m m = 1.2 f(x) = -0.1x 2 + 1.2x + 98.6 f'(x) = -0.2x + 1.2 = -0.2(x - 6) In the Interval f'(x) Conclusion (0, 6) +Ve f is strictly increasing in [0, 6] (6, 12) -Ve f is strictly decreasing in [6, 12] OR f(x) = -0.1x 2 + 1.2x + 98.6, f'(x) = -0.2x + 1.2, f'(6) = 0, f"(x) = -0.2 f"(6) = -0.2 < 0 Hence, by second derivative test 6 is a point of local maximum. The local maximum value = f(6) = -0.1 {tex}\times {/tex} 6 2 + 1.2 {tex}\times {/tex} 6 + 98.6 = 102.2 We have f(0) = 98.6, f(6) = 102.2, f(12) = 98.6 6 is the point of absolute maximum and the absolute maximum value of the function = 102.2. 0 and 12 both are the points of absolute minimum and the absolute minimum value of the function = 98.6.
solution_html:
1. f(x) = -0.1x2 + mx + 98.6, being a polynomial function, is differentiable everywhere, hence, differentiable in (0, 12).

  2. f(x) = -0.2x + m
    At Critical point
    0 = -0.2 {tex}\times{/tex} 6 + m
    m = 1.2

  3. f(x) = -0.1x2 + 1.2x + 98.6
    f'(x) = -0.2x + 1.2 = -0.2(x - 6)

    In the Interval f'(x) Conclusion
    (0, 6) +Ve f is strictly increasing in [0, 6]
    (6, 12) -Ve f is strictly decreasing in [6, 12]

OR 
f(x) = -0.1x2 + 1.2x + 98.6,
f'(x) = -0.2x + 1.2, f'(6) = 0,
f"(x) = -0.2
f"(6) = -0.2 < 0
Hence, by second derivative test 6 is a point of local maximum. The local maximum value = f(6) = -0.1 {tex}\times {/tex} 62 + 1.2 {tex}\times {/tex} 6 + 98.6 = 102.2
We have f(0) = 98.6, f(6) = 102.2, f(12) = 98.6
6 is the point of absolute maximum and the absolute maximum value of the function = 102.2.
0 and 12 both are the points of absolute minimum and the absolute minimum value of the function = 98.6.

question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
The Government declare that farmers can get ₹300 per quintal for their onions on 1st July and after that, the price will be dropped by ₹3 per quintal per extra day. Govind's father has 80 quintals of onions in the field on 1st July and he estimates that the crop is increasing at the rate of 1 quintal per day.

  1. If x is the number of days after 1st July, then express price and quantity of onion and the revenue as a function of x. (1)
  2. Find the number of days after 1st July, when Govind's father attains maximum revenue. (1)
  3. On which day should Govind's father harvest the onions to maximize his revenue? (2)
    OR
    Find the maximum revenue collected by Govind's father. (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: The Government declare that farmers can get ₹300 per quintal for their onions on 1st July and after that, the price will be dropped by ₹3 per quintal per extra day. Govind's father has 80 quintals of onions in the field on 1st July and he estimates that the crop is increasing at the rate of 1 quintal per day. If x is the number of days after 1 st July, then express price and quantity of onion and the revenue as a function of x. (1) Find the number of days after 1st July, when Govind's father attains maximum revenue. (1) On which day should Govind's father harvest the onions to maximize his revenue? (2) OR Find the maximum revenue collected by Govind's father. (2)
explanation: Let x be the number of extra days after 1st July. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Price = ₹(300 - 3 {tex}\times{/tex} x) = ₹(300 - 3x) Quantity = 80 quintals + x(1 quintal per day) = (80 + x) quintals Revenue = R(x) = Quantity {tex}\times{/tex} Price = (80 + x) (300 - 3x) = 24000 - 240x + 300x - 3x 2 R(x) = 24000 + 60x - 3x 2 We have, R(x) - 24000 + 60x - 3x 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R'(x) = 60 - 6x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R"(x) = -6 For R(x) to be maximum, R'(x) = 0 and R"(x) < 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 60 - 6x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 10 Govind's father will attain maximum revenue after 10 days. So, he should harvest the onions after 10 days of 1st July i.e., on 11th July OR Maximum revenue is collected by Govind's father when x = 10 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Maximum revenue = R(10) = 24000 + 60(10) - 3(10) 2 = 24000 + 600 - 300 = ₹24,300
solution: Let x be the number of extra days after 1st July. {tex}\therefore{/tex} Price = ₹(300 - 3 {tex}\times{/tex} x) = ₹(300 - 3x) Quantity = 80 quintals + x(1 quintal per day) = (80 + x) quintals Revenue = R(x) = Quantity {tex}\times{/tex} Price = (80 + x) (300 - 3x) = 24000 - 240x + 300x - 3x 2 R(x) = 24000 + 60x - 3x 2 We have, R(x) - 24000 + 60x - 3x 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R'(x) = 60 - 6x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R"(x) = -6 For R(x) to be maximum, R'(x) = 0 and R"(x) < 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 60 - 6x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 10 Govind's father will attain maximum revenue after 10 days. So, he should harvest the onions after 10 days of 1st July i.e., on 11th July OR Maximum revenue is collected by Govind's father when x = 10 {tex}\therefore{/tex} Maximum revenue = R(10) = 24000 + 60(10) - 3(10) 2 = 24000 + 600 - 300 = ₹24,300
solution_html:
1. Let x be the number of extra days after 1st July.
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Price = ₹(300 - 3 {tex}\times{/tex} x) = ₹(300 - 3x)
    Quantity = 80 quintals + x(1 quintal per day) = (80 + x) quintals
    Revenue = R(x) = Quantity {tex}\times{/tex} Price = (80 + x) (300 - 3x) = 24000 - 240x + 300x - 3x2
    R(x) = 24000 + 60x - 3x2
  2. We have, R(x) - 24000 + 60x - 3x2
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R'(x) = 60 - 6x {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} R"(x) = -6
    For R(x) to be maximum, R'(x) = 0 and R"(x) < 0
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 60 - 6x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 10
  3. Govind's father will attain maximum revenue after 10 days.
    So, he should harvest the onions after 10 days of 1st July i.e., on 11th July
    OR
    Maximum revenue is collected by Govind's father when x = 10
    {tex}\therefore{/tex} Maximum revenue = R(10)
    = 24000 + 60(10) - 3(10)2 = 24000 + 600 - 300 = ₹24,300

A window is in the form of a rectangle surmounted by an equilateral triangle on its length. Let the rectangular part have length and breadth x and y metres respectively.
Based on the given information, answer the following questions:

  1. If the perimeter of the window is 12 m, find the relation between x and y. (1)
  2. Using the expression obtained in(i), write an expression for the area of the window as a function of {tex}x{/tex} only. (1)
    1. Find the dimensions of the rectangle that will allow maximum light through the window. (use expression obtained in(ii)) (2)

      OR

    2. If it is given that the area of the window is 50 m2, find an expression for its perimeter in terms of x. (2)
question_text: A window is in the form of a rectangle surmounted by an equilateral triangle on its length. Let the rectangular part have length and breadth x and y metres respectively. Based on the given information, answer the following questions: If the perimeter of the window is 12 m, find the relation between x and y. (1) Using the expression obtained in(i), write an expression for the area of the window as a function of {tex}x{/tex} only. (1) Find the dimensions of the rectangle that will allow maximum light through the window. (use expression obtained in(ii)) (2) OR If it is given that the area of the window is 50 m 2 , find an expression for its perimeter in terms of x. (2)
explanation: Perimeter {tex}(P)=3 x+2 y=12{/tex} {tex}\operatorname{Area}(A)=x y+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} x^{2}{/tex} {tex}=x\left(\frac{12-3 x}{2}\right)+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} x^{2}{/tex} {tex}=6 x-\frac{3}{2} x^{2}+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} x^{2}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d A}{d x}=6-3 x+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} x{/tex} For maximum light, {tex}\frac{d A}{d x}=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 6-3 x+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} x=0 \Rightarrow x=\frac{12}{6-\sqrt{3}} m{/tex} Also, {tex}\frac{d^{2} A}{d x^{2}}=-3+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \lt 0 \therefore A{/tex} is maximum when {tex}x=\frac{12}{6-\sqrt{3}} m{/tex} Now, {tex}y=\frac{12-3 x}{2}=6-\frac{3}{2}\left(\frac{12}{6-\sqrt{3}}\right)=\frac{18-6 \sqrt{3}}{6-\sqrt{3}} {~m}{/tex} OR {tex}x y+\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} x^{2}=50{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=\frac{50}{x}-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} x{/tex} Now, {tex}P=3 x+2 y{/tex} {tex}=3 x+2\left(\frac{50}{x}-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4} x\right) m{/tex}

A ladder of fixed length '{tex}h{/tex}' is to be placed along the wall such that it is free to move along the height of the wall.
Based upon the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. Express the distance (y) between the wall and foot of the ladder in terms of '{tex}h{/tex}' and height ( {tex}x{/tex} ) on the wall at a certain instant. Also, write an expression in terms of {tex}h{/tex} and {tex}x{/tex} for the area (A) of the right triangle, as seen from the side by an observer. (1)
  2. Find the derivative of the area (A) with respect to the height on the wall (x), and find its critical point. (1)
    1. Show that the area (A) of the right triangle is maximum at the critical point. (2)

      OR

    2. If the foot of the ladder whose length is 5 m , is being pulled towards the wall such that the rate of decrease of distance (y) is {tex}2 {~m} / {s}{/tex}, then at what rate is the height on the wall (x) increasing, when the foot of the ladder is 3 m away from the wall? (2)
question_text: A ladder of fixed length ' {tex}h{/tex} ' is to be placed along the wall such that it is free to move along the height of the wall. Based upon the above information, answer the following questions: Express the distance (y) between the wall and foot of the ladder in terms of ' {tex}h{/tex} ' and height ( {tex}x{/tex} ) on the wall at a certain instant. Also, write an expression in terms of {tex}h{/tex} and {tex}x{/tex} for the area (A) of the right triangle, as seen from the side by an observer. (1) Find the derivative of the area (A) with respect to the height on the wall (x), and find its critical point. (1) Show that the area (A) of the right triangle is maximum at the critical point. (2) OR If the foot of the ladder whose length is 5 m , is being pulled towards the wall such that the rate of decrease of distance (y) is {tex}2 {~m} / {s}{/tex} , then at what rate is the height on the wall (x) increasing, when the foot of the ladder is 3 m away from the wall? (2)
explanation: {tex}y^{2}=h^{2}-x^{2}{/tex} {tex}A=\frac{1}{2} x y=\frac{1}{2} x \sqrt{h^{2}-x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d A}{d x}=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{h^{2}-x^{2}}+\frac{1}{2} x \frac{-x}{\sqrt{h^{2}-x^{2}}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dA}}{{dx}}=0{/tex} gives {tex}{x}=\frac{{h}}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}-4 x \cdot \sqrt{h^{2}-x^{2}}-\left(h^{2}-2 x^{2}\right) \frac{-x}{\sqrt{h^{2}-x^{2}}}{/tex} {tex}{A}^{\prime \prime}=\frac{1}{2} {~h}^{2}-{x}^{2} \sqrt{{~h}^{2}-{x}^{2}}{/tex} is {tex} \lt 0{/tex} at {tex}x=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} Hence A is maximum at critical point OR {tex}{y}^{2}=25-{x}^{2}{/tex} hence {tex}{y}=3{/tex} gives {tex}{x}=4{/tex} {tex}2 y \frac{d y}{d t}=-2 x \frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}=1.5 {~m} / {s}{/tex}
solution: {tex}y^{2}=h^{2}-x^{2}{/tex} {tex}A=\frac{1}{2} x y=\frac{1}{2} x \sqrt{h^{2}-x^{2}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{d A}{d x}=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{h^{2}-x^{2}}+\frac{1}{2} x \frac{-x}{\sqrt{h^{2}-x^{2}}}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dA}}{{dx}}=0{/tex} gives {tex}{x}=\frac{{h}}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} {tex}-4 x \cdot \sqrt{h^{2}-x^{2}}-\left(h^{2}-2 x^{2}\right) \frac{-x}{\sqrt{h^{2}-x^{2}}}{/tex} {tex}{A}^{\prime \prime}=\frac{1}{2} {~h}^{2}-{x}^{2} \sqrt{{~h}^{2}-{x}^{2}}{/tex} is {tex} \lt 0{/tex} at {tex}x=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex} Hence A is maximum at critical point OR {tex}{y}^{2}=25-{x}^{2}{/tex} hence {tex}{y}=3{/tex} gives {tex}{x}=4{/tex} {tex}2 y \frac{d y}{d t}=-2 x \frac{d x}{d t}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}=1.5 {~m} / {s}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex}y^{2}=h^{2}-x^{2}{/tex}
    {tex}A=\frac{1}{2} x y=\frac{1}{2} x \sqrt{h^{2}-x^{2}}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\frac{d A}{d x}=\frac{1}{2} \sqrt{h^{2}-x^{2}}+\frac{1}{2} x \frac{-x}{\sqrt{h^{2}-x^{2}}}{/tex}
    {tex}\frac{{dA}}{{dx}}=0{/tex} gives {tex}{x}=\frac{{h}}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
    {tex}-4 x \cdot \sqrt{h^{2}-x^{2}}-\left(h^{2}-2 x^{2}\right) \frac{-x}{\sqrt{h^{2}-x^{2}}}{/tex}
    1. {tex}{A}^{\prime \prime}=\frac{1}{2} {~h}^{2}-{x}^{2} \sqrt{{~h}^{2}-{x}^{2}}{/tex} is {tex} \lt 0{/tex} at {tex}x=\frac{h}{\sqrt{2}}{/tex}
      Hence A is maximum at critical point

      OR

    2. {tex}{y}^{2}=25-{x}^{2}{/tex} hence {tex}{y}=3{/tex} gives {tex}{x}=4{/tex}
      {tex}2 y \frac{d y}{d t}=-2 x \frac{d x}{d t}{/tex}
      {tex}\frac{{dx}}{{dt}}=1.5 {~m} / {s}{/tex}
