question_html:
Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow:
Engine displacement is the measure of the cylinder volume swept by all the pistons of a piston engine. The piston moves inside the cylinder bore

The cylinder bore in the form of circular cylinder open at the top is to be made from a metal sheet of area {tex}75\pi{/tex} cm2.

  1. If the radius of cylinder is r cm and height is h cm, then write the volume V of cylinder in terms of radius r. (1)
  2. Find {tex}\frac {\text {dV}}{\text {dr}}{/tex}. (1)
  3. Find the radius of cylinder when its volume is maximum. (2)
    OR
    For maximum volume, h > r. State true or false and justify. (2)
question_text: Read the following text carefully and answer the questions that follow: Engine displacement is the measure of the cylinder volume swept by all the pistons of a piston engine. The piston moves inside the cylinder bore The cylinder bore in the form of circular cylinder open at the top is to be made from a metal sheet of area {tex}75\pi{/tex} cm 2 . If the radius of cylinder is r cm and height is h cm, then write the volume V of cylinder in terms of radius r. (1) Find {tex}\frac {\text {dV}}{\text {dr}}{/tex} . (1) Find the radius of cylinder when its volume is maximum. (2) OR For maximum volume, h > r. State true or false and justify. (2)
explanation: {tex}\pi r^2{/tex} + {tex}2 \pi r h{/tex} = {tex}75 \pi{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = {tex}\frac{75-r^2}{2 r}{/tex} , {tex}\therefore{/tex} V = {tex}\pi r^2 h{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} (75r - r 3 ) {tex}\frac {\text {dV}}{\text {dr}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac \pi 2{/tex} (75 - 3r 2 ) {tex}\left.\frac{\text {dV}}{\text {dr}}=0 \Rightarrow \text r=5, \frac{\text d^2 \text V}{\text {dr}^2}\right]_{\text r=5}{/tex} = {tex}\frac \pi 2{/tex} (-6r) < 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} volume is maximum when r = 5 OR False, {tex}\left.\frac{\text {dV}}{\text {dr}}=0 \Rightarrow \text r=5, \frac{\text d^2 \text V}{\text {dr}^2}\right]_{\mathrm{r}=5}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} (-6r) < 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} volume is maximum when r = 5 As volume is maximum at r = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = {tex}\frac{75-5^2}{2(5)}{/tex} = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = r
solution: {tex}\pi r^2{/tex} + {tex}2 \pi r h{/tex} = {tex}75 \pi{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = {tex}\frac{75-r^2}{2 r}{/tex} , {tex}\therefore{/tex} V = {tex}\pi r^2 h{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} (75r - r 3 ) {tex}\frac {\text {dV}}{\text {dr}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac \pi 2{/tex} (75 - 3r 2 ) {tex}\left.\frac{\text {dV}}{\text {dr}}=0 \Rightarrow \text r=5, \frac{\text d^2 \text V}{\text {dr}^2}\right]_{\text r=5}{/tex} = {tex}\frac \pi 2{/tex} (-6r) < 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} volume is maximum when r = 5 OR False, {tex}\left.\frac{\text {dV}}{\text {dr}}=0 \Rightarrow \text r=5, \frac{\text d^2 \text V}{\text {dr}^2}\right]_{\mathrm{r}=5}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex} (-6r) < 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} volume is maximum when r = 5 As volume is maximum at r = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = {tex}\frac{75-5^2}{2(5)}{/tex} = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = r
solution_html:
1. {tex}\pi r^2{/tex} + {tex}2 \pi r h{/tex} = {tex}75 \pi{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = {tex}\frac{75-r^2}{2 r}{/tex}, {tex}\therefore{/tex} V = {tex}\pi r^2 h{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}(75r - r3)
  2. {tex}\frac {\text {dV}}{\text {dr}}{/tex} = {tex}\frac \pi 2{/tex}(75 - 3r2)
  3. {tex}\left.\frac{\text {dV}}{\text {dr}}=0 \Rightarrow \text r=5, \frac{\text d^2 \text V}{\text {dr}^2}\right]_{\text r=5}{/tex} = {tex}\frac \pi 2{/tex}(-6r) < 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} volume is maximum when r = 5
    OR
    False,
    {tex}\left.\frac{\text {dV}}{\text {dr}}=0 \Rightarrow \text r=5, \frac{\text d^2 \text V}{\text {dr}^2}\right]_{\mathrm{r}=5}{/tex} = {tex}\frac{\pi}{2}{/tex}(-6r) < 0 {tex}\therefore{/tex} volume is maximum when r = 5
    As volume is maximum at r = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = {tex}\frac{75-5^2}{2(5)}{/tex} = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = r

question_html:
In an agricultural institute, scientists do experiments with varieties of seeds to grow them in different environments to produce healthy plants and get more yield.
A scientist observed that a particular seed grew very fast after germination. He had recorded growth of plant since germination and he said that its growth can be defined by the function
f(x) = {tex}\frac 13{/tex}x3 - 4x2 + 15x + 2, 0 {tex}\leq{/tex} x {tex}\leq{/tex} 10
where x is the number of days the plant is exposed to sunlight.

On the basis of the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. What are the critical points of the function f(x)? (2)
  2. Using second derivative test, find the minimum value of the function. (2)
question_text: In an agricultural institute, scientists do experiments with varieties of seeds to grow them in different environments to produce healthy plants and get more yield. A scientist observed that a particular seed grew very fast after germination. He had recorded growth of plant since germination and he said that its growth can be defined by the function f(x) = {tex}\frac 13{/tex} x 3 - 4x 2 + 15x + 2, 0 {tex}\leq{/tex} x {tex}\leq{/tex} 10 where x is the number of days the plant is exposed to sunlight. On the basis of the above information, answer the following questions: What are the critical points of the function f(x)? (2) Using second derivative test, find the minimum value of the function. (2)
explanation: f l (x) = x 2 - 8x + 15 = (x - 3)(x - 5) f l (x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 3, 5 are the critical points. Now f II (x) = 2x - 8 f ll (3) < 0 and f ll (5) > 0 so, mimimum value of f(x) is at x = 5. min. value = f(5) = {tex}\frac{5^3}{3}{/tex} - 4(5) 2 + 15(5) + 2 = {tex}\frac{56}{3} {/tex}
solution: f l (x) = x 2 - 8x + 15 = (x - 3)(x - 5) f l (x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 3, 5 are the critical points. Now f II (x) = 2x - 8 f ll (3) < 0 and f ll (5) > 0 so, mimimum value of f(x) is at x = 5. min. value = f(5) = {tex}\frac{5^3}{3}{/tex} - 4(5) 2 + 15(5) + 2 = {tex}\frac{56}{3} {/tex}
solution_html:
1. fl(x) = x2 - 8x + 15 = (x - 3)(x - 5)
    fl(x) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 3, 5 are the critical points.
  2. Now fII(x) = 2x - 8
    fll(3) < 0 and fll(5) > 0
    so, mimimum value of f(x) is at x = 5.
    min. value = f(5) = {tex}\frac{5^3}{3}{/tex} - 4(5)2 + 15(5) + 2 = {tex}\frac{56}{3} {/tex}

question_html:
A housing society wants to commission a swimming pool for its residents. For this, they have to purchase a square piece of land and dig this to such a depth that its capacity is 250 cubic metres. Cost of land is ₹ 500 per square metre. The cost of digging increases with the depth and cost for the whole pool is ₹ 4000 (depth)2.

Suppose the side of the square plot is x metres and depth is h metres.
On the basis of the above information, answer the following questions:

  1. Write cost C(h) as a function in terms of h. (1)
  2. Find critical point. (1)
  3. Use second derivative test to find the value of h for which cost of constructing the pool is minimum. What is the minimum cost of construction of the pool? (2)

OR

  1. Use first derivative test to find the depth of the pool so that cost of construction is minimum. Also, find relation between x and h for minimum cost. (2)
question_text: A housing society wants to commission a swimming pool for its residents. For this, they have to purchase a square piece of land and dig this to such a depth that its capacity is 250 cubic metres. Cost of land is ₹ 500 per square metre. The cost of digging increases with the depth and cost for the whole pool is ₹ 4000 (depth) 2 . Suppose the side of the square plot is x metres and depth is h metres. On the basis of the above information, answer the following questions: Write cost C(h) as a function in terms of h. (1) Find critical point. (1) Use second derivative test to find the value of h for which cost of constructing the pool is minimum. What is the minimum cost of construction of the pool? (2) OR Use first derivative test to find the depth of the pool so that cost of construction is minimum. Also, find relation between x and h for minimum cost. (2)
explanation: Capacity = area {tex}\times{/tex} depth = x 2 h = 250 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 = {tex}\frac{250}{h}{/tex} C(cost ) = 500x 2 + 4000h 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = {tex}500\left(\frac{250}{h}\right){/tex} + 4000h 2 = {tex}\frac{125000}{h}{/tex} + 4000h 2 {tex}\frac{\text {dC}}{\text {dh}}=-\frac{125000}{h^2}{/tex} + 8000h {tex}\frac{\text {dC}}{\text {dh}}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} m or 2.5 m {tex}\frac{d^2 C}{d h^2}{/tex} = {tex}-125000\left(\frac{-2}{h^3}\right){/tex} + 8000 = {tex}\frac{250000}{h^3}{/tex} + 8000 {tex}\left.\frac{\text d^2 \text C}{\text {dh}^2}\right|_{\text h = 2.5 \text m}{/tex} > 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Cost is minimum when h = 2.5 m Minimum cost = C = {tex}\frac{125000}{\left(\frac{5}{2}\right)}+4000\left(\frac{5}{2}\right)^2{/tex} = ₹ 75,000 OR we already have found above that h = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} m when {tex}\frac{d C}{d h}{/tex} = 0 for the values of h less than {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} and close to {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} , {tex}\frac{d C}{d h}{/tex} < 0 and, for the values of h more than {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} and close to {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} , {tex}\frac{d C}{d h}{/tex} > 0 By first derivative test, there is a minimum at h = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} Now, x 2 = {tex}\frac{250}{h} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 = {tex}\frac{250}{\left(\frac{5}{2}\right)}{/tex} = 100 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 10 m also, x = 4h
solution: Capacity = area {tex}\times{/tex} depth = x 2 h = 250 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 = {tex}\frac{250}{h}{/tex} C(cost ) = 500x 2 + 4000h 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = {tex}500\left(\frac{250}{h}\right){/tex} + 4000h 2 = {tex}\frac{125000}{h}{/tex} + 4000h 2 {tex}\frac{\text {dC}}{\text {dh}}=-\frac{125000}{h^2}{/tex} + 8000h {tex}\frac{\text {dC}}{\text {dh}}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} m or 2.5 m {tex}\frac{d^2 C}{d h^2}{/tex} = {tex}-125000\left(\frac{-2}{h^3}\right){/tex} + 8000 = {tex}\frac{250000}{h^3}{/tex} + 8000 {tex}\left.\frac{\text d^2 \text C}{\text {dh}^2}\right|_{\text h = 2.5 \text m}{/tex} > 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Cost is minimum when h = 2.5 m Minimum cost = C = {tex}\frac{125000}{\left(\frac{5}{2}\right)}+4000\left(\frac{5}{2}\right)^2{/tex} = ₹ 75,000 OR we already have found above that h = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} m when {tex}\frac{d C}{d h}{/tex} = 0 for the values of h less than {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} and close to {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} , {tex}\frac{d C}{d h}{/tex} < 0 and, for the values of h more than {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} and close to {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} , {tex}\frac{d C}{d h}{/tex} > 0 By first derivative test, there is a minimum at h = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} Now, x 2 = {tex}\frac{250}{h} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 = {tex}\frac{250}{\left(\frac{5}{2}\right)}{/tex} = 100 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 10 m also, x = 4h
solution_html:
1. Capacity = area {tex}\times{/tex} depth = x2h = 250 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 = {tex}\frac{250}{h}{/tex}
    C(cost ) = 500x2 + 4000h2
    {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} C = {tex}500\left(\frac{250}{h}\right){/tex} + 4000h2 = {tex}\frac{125000}{h}{/tex} + 4000h2
  2. {tex}\frac{\text {dC}}{\text {dh}}=-\frac{125000}{h^2}{/tex} + 8000h
    {tex}\frac{\text {dC}}{\text {dh}}{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} h = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} m or 2.5 m
  3. {tex}\frac{d^2 C}{d h^2}{/tex} = {tex}-125000\left(\frac{-2}{h^3}\right){/tex} + 8000 = {tex}\frac{250000}{h^3}{/tex} + 8000
    {tex}\left.\frac{\text d^2 \text C}{\text {dh}^2}\right|_{\text h = 2.5 \text m}{/tex} > 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Cost is minimum when h = 2.5 m
    Minimum cost = C = {tex}\frac{125000}{\left(\frac{5}{2}\right)}+4000\left(\frac{5}{2}\right)^2{/tex} = ₹ 75,000

OR

  1. we already have found above that h = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} m when {tex}\frac{d C}{d h}{/tex} = 0
    for the values of h less than {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} and close to {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{d C}{d h}{/tex} < 0
    and, for the values of h more than {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex} and close to {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex}, {tex}\frac{d C}{d h}{/tex} > 0
    By first derivative test, there is a minimum at h = {tex}\frac{5}{2}{/tex}
    Now, x2 = {tex}\frac{250}{h} {/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 = {tex}\frac{250}{\left(\frac{5}{2}\right)}{/tex} = 100 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 10 m
    also, x = 4h

question_html:
A small town is analyzing the pattern of a new street light installation. The lights are set up in such a way that the intensity of light at any point {tex}x{/tex} metres from the start of the street can be modelled by {tex}{f}(x)={e}^{x} \sin x{/tex}, where {tex}x{/tex} is in metres.
Based on the above, answer the following:

  1. Find the intervals on which the {tex}{f}(x){/tex} is increasing or decreasing, {tex}x \in[0, \pi]{/tex}. (2)
  2. Verify, whether each critical point when {tex}x \in[0, \pi]{/tex} is a point of local maximum or local minimum or a point of inflexion. (2)
question_text: A small town is analyzing the pattern of a new street light installation. The lights are set up in such a way that the intensity of light at any point {tex}x{/tex} metres from the start of the street can be modelled by {tex}{f}(x)={e}^{x} \sin x{/tex} , where {tex}x{/tex} is in metres. Based on the above, answer the following: Find the intervals on which the {tex}{f}(x){/tex} is increasing or decreasing, {tex}x \in[0, \pi]{/tex} . (2) Verify, whether each critical point when {tex}x \in[0, \pi]{/tex} is a point of local maximum or local minimum or a point of inflexion. (2)
explanation: {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=e^{x}(\cos x+\sin x){/tex} For critical points, {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos x+\sin x=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos x=-\sin x{/tex} For x to be a critical point {tex}x \in(0, \pi){/tex} , hence, {tex}x=\frac{3 \pi}{4}{/tex} For all {tex}x \in\left[0, \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right], f^{\prime}(x) \geq 0{/tex} Hence, {tex}f{/tex} is increasing in {tex}\left[0, \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left(0, \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right){/tex} or {tex}\left[0, \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right){/tex} or {tex}\left(0, \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right]{/tex} For all {tex}x \in\left[\frac{3 \pi}{4}, \pi\right], f^{\prime}(x) \leq 0{/tex} Hence, f is decreasing in {tex}\left[\frac{3 \pi}{4}, \pi\right]{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{3 \pi}{4}, \pi\right){/tex} or {tex}\left(\frac{3 \pi}{4}, \pi\right]{/tex} or {tex}\left[\frac{3 \pi}{4}, \pi\right){/tex} {tex}x=\frac{3 \pi}{4}{/tex} is a critical point {tex}f^{\prime \prime}(x)=e^{x}(\cos x-\sin x)+e^{x}(\cos x+\sin x){/tex} {tex}=2 e^{x} \cos x{/tex} {tex}f^{\prime \prime}\left(\frac{3 \pi}{4}\right)=-v e{/tex} Hence, {tex}\frac{3 \pi}{4}{/tex} is a point of local maximum.
solution: {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=e^{x}(\cos x+\sin x){/tex} For critical points, {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos x+\sin x=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \cos x=-\sin x{/tex} For x to be a critical point {tex}x \in(0, \pi){/tex} , hence, {tex}x=\frac{3 \pi}{4}{/tex} For all {tex}x \in\left[0, \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right], f^{\prime}(x) \geq 0{/tex} Hence, {tex}f{/tex} is increasing in {tex}\left[0, \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left(0, \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right){/tex} or {tex}\left[0, \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right){/tex} or {tex}\left(0, \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right]{/tex} For all {tex}x \in\left[\frac{3 \pi}{4}, \pi\right], f^{\prime}(x) \leq 0{/tex} Hence, f is decreasing in {tex}\left[\frac{3 \pi}{4}, \pi\right]{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{3 \pi}{4}, \pi\right){/tex} or {tex}\left(\frac{3 \pi}{4}, \pi\right]{/tex} or {tex}\left[\frac{3 \pi}{4}, \pi\right){/tex} {tex}x=\frac{3 \pi}{4}{/tex} is a critical point {tex}f^{\prime \prime}(x)=e^{x}(\cos x-\sin x)+e^{x}(\cos x+\sin x){/tex} {tex}=2 e^{x} \cos x{/tex} {tex}f^{\prime \prime}\left(\frac{3 \pi}{4}\right)=-v e{/tex} Hence, {tex}\frac{3 \pi}{4}{/tex} is a point of local maximum.
solution_html:
1. {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=e^{x}(\cos x+\sin x){/tex}
    For critical points, {tex}f^{\prime}(x)=0{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \cos x+\sin x=0{/tex}
    {tex}\Rightarrow \cos x=-\sin x{/tex}
    For x to be a critical point {tex}x \in(0, \pi){/tex}, hence, {tex}x=\frac{3 \pi}{4}{/tex}
    For all {tex}x \in\left[0, \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right], f^{\prime}(x) \geq 0{/tex}
    Hence, {tex}f{/tex} is increasing in {tex}\left[0, \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\left(0, \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right){/tex} or {tex}\left[0, \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right){/tex} or {tex}\left(0, \frac{3 \pi}{4}\right]{/tex}
    For all {tex}x \in\left[\frac{3 \pi}{4}, \pi\right], f^{\prime}(x) \leq 0{/tex}
    Hence, f is decreasing in {tex}\left[\frac{3 \pi}{4}, \pi\right]{/tex}
    {tex}\left(\frac{3 \pi}{4}, \pi\right){/tex} or {tex}\left(\frac{3 \pi}{4}, \pi\right]{/tex} or {tex}\left[\frac{3 \pi}{4}, \pi\right){/tex}
  2. {tex}x=\frac{3 \pi}{4}{/tex} is a critical point
    {tex}f^{\prime \prime}(x)=e^{x}(\cos x-\sin x)+e^{x}(\cos x+\sin x){/tex}
    {tex}=2 e^{x} \cos x{/tex}
    {tex}f^{\prime \prime}\left(\frac{3 \pi}{4}\right)=-v e{/tex}
    Hence, {tex}\frac{3 \pi}{4}{/tex} is a point of local maximum.

question_html:
A carpenter needs to make a wooden cuboidal box, closed from all sides, which has a square base and fixed volume. Since he is short of the paint required to paint the box on completion, he wants the surface area to be minimum.
On the basis of the above information, answer the following questions :

  1. Taking length = breadth = x m and height = y m, express the surface area (S) of the box in terms of x and its volume (V), which is constant. (1)
  2. Find {tex}\frac{{dS}}{{dx}}{/tex}. (1)
    1. Find a relation between x and y such that the surface area (S) is minimum. (2)

      OR

    2. If surface area (S) is constant, the volume (V) {tex}=\frac{1}{4}\left({Sx}-2 {x}^{3}\right){/tex}, x being the edge of base. Show that volume ( V ) is maximum for {tex}{x}=\sqrt{\frac{{S}}{6}}{/tex}. (2)
question_text: A carpenter needs to make a wooden cuboidal box, closed from all sides, which has a square base and fixed volume. Since he is short of the paint required to paint the box on completion, he wants the surface area to be minimum. On the basis of the above information, answer the following questions : Taking length = breadth = x m and height = y m, express the surface area (S) of the box in terms of x and its volume (V), which is constant. (1) Find {tex}\frac{{dS}}{{dx}}{/tex} . (1) Find a relation between x and y such that the surface area (S) is minimum. (2) OR If surface area (S) is constant, the volume (V) {tex}=\frac{1}{4}\left({Sx}-2 {x}^{3}\right){/tex} , x being the edge of base. Show that volume ( V ) is maximum for {tex}{x}=\sqrt{\frac{{S}}{6}}{/tex} . (2)
explanation: {tex}V=x^{2} y \Rightarrow y=\frac{V}{x^{2}}{/tex} ...(I) Hence, {tex}S=2 x^{2}+4 x y=2 x^{2}+\frac{4 V}{x}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dS}}{{dx}}=4\left({x}-\frac{{V}}{{x}^{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{d S}{d x}=0 \Rightarrow V=x^{3} \Rightarrow x^{2} y=x^{3} \Rightarrow y{/tex} {tex}=x \frac{{d}^{2} {~s}}{{dx}^{2}}=4\left(1+\frac{2 {~V}}{{x}^{3}}\right) \gt 0 \Rightarrow S{/tex} is minimum if {tex}y=x{/tex} . OR {tex}{V}=\frac{1}{4}\left({Sx}-2 {x}^{3}\right) \Rightarrow \frac{{dV}}{{dx}}=\frac{1}{4}\left({~S}-6 {x}^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\text { Put } \frac{d V}{d x}=0 \Rightarrow x=\sqrt{\frac{s}{6}}{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{d^{2} V}{d x^{2}}\right)_{x=\sqrt{\frac{s}{6}}}=-3 \sqrt{\frac{s}{6}} \lt 0 \Rightarrow \text { Volume is maximum for } x=\sqrt{\frac{s}{6}}{/tex}
solution: {tex}V=x^{2} y \Rightarrow y=\frac{V}{x^{2}}{/tex} ...(I) Hence, {tex}S=2 x^{2}+4 x y=2 x^{2}+\frac{4 V}{x}{/tex} {tex}\frac{{dS}}{{dx}}=4\left({x}-\frac{{V}}{{x}^{2}}\right){/tex} {tex}\frac{d S}{d x}=0 \Rightarrow V=x^{3} \Rightarrow x^{2} y=x^{3} \Rightarrow y{/tex} {tex}=x \frac{{d}^{2} {~s}}{{dx}^{2}}=4\left(1+\frac{2 {~V}}{{x}^{3}}\right) \gt 0 \Rightarrow S{/tex} is minimum if {tex}y=x{/tex} . OR {tex}{V}=\frac{1}{4}\left({Sx}-2 {x}^{3}\right) \Rightarrow \frac{{dV}}{{dx}}=\frac{1}{4}\left({~S}-6 {x}^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\text { Put } \frac{d V}{d x}=0 \Rightarrow x=\sqrt{\frac{s}{6}}{/tex} {tex}\left(\frac{d^{2} V}{d x^{2}}\right)_{x=\sqrt{\frac{s}{6}}}=-3 \sqrt{\frac{s}{6}} \lt 0 \Rightarrow \text { Volume is maximum for } x=\sqrt{\frac{s}{6}}{/tex}
solution_html:
1. {tex}V=x^{2} y \Rightarrow y=\frac{V}{x^{2}}{/tex} ...(I)
    Hence, {tex}S=2 x^{2}+4 x y=2 x^{2}+\frac{4 V}{x}{/tex}
  2. {tex}\frac{{dS}}{{dx}}=4\left({x}-\frac{{V}}{{x}^{2}}\right){/tex}
    1. {tex}\frac{d S}{d x}=0 \Rightarrow V=x^{3} \Rightarrow x^{2} y=x^{3} \Rightarrow y{/tex} {tex}=x \frac{{d}^{2} {~s}}{{dx}^{2}}=4\left(1+\frac{2 {~V}}{{x}^{3}}\right) \gt 0 \Rightarrow S{/tex} is minimum if {tex}y=x{/tex}.

      OR

    2. {tex}{V}=\frac{1}{4}\left({Sx}-2 {x}^{3}\right) \Rightarrow \frac{{dV}}{{dx}}=\frac{1}{4}\left({~S}-6 {x}^{2}\right){/tex}
      {tex}\text { Put } \frac{d V}{d x}=0 \Rightarrow x=\sqrt{\frac{s}{6}}{/tex}
      {tex}\left(\frac{d^{2} V}{d x^{2}}\right)_{x=\sqrt{\frac{s}{6}}}=-3 \sqrt{\frac{s}{6}} \lt 0 \Rightarrow \text { Volume is maximum for } x=\sqrt{\frac{s}{6}}{/tex}

A technical company is designing a rectangular solar panel installation on a roof using 300 metres of boundary material. The design includes a partition running parallel to one of the sides dividing the area (roof) into two sections.
Let the length of the side perpendicular to the partition be {tex}x{/tex} metres and with parallel to the partition be {tex}y{/tex} metres.
Based on this information, answer the following questions:

  1. Write the equation for the total boundary material used in the boundary and parallel to the partition in terms of {tex}x{/tex} and {tex}y{/tex}. (1)
  2. Write the area of the solar panel as a function of {tex}x{/tex}. (1)
    1. Find the critical points of the area function. Use second derivative test to determine critical points at the maximum area. Also, find the maximum area. (2)

      OR

    2. Using first derivative test, calculate the maximum area the company can enclose with the 300 metres of boundary material, considering the parallel partition. (2)
question_text: A technical company is designing a rectangular solar panel installation on a roof using 300 metres of boundary material. The design includes a partition running parallel to one of the sides dividing the area (roof) into two sections. Let the length of the side perpendicular to the partition be {tex}x{/tex} metres and with parallel to the partition be {tex}y{/tex} metres. (1) Write the area of the solar panel as a function of {tex}x{/tex} . (1) Find the critical points of the area function. Use second derivative test to determine critical points at the maximum area. Also, find the maximum area. (2) OR Using first derivative test, calculate the maximum area the company can enclose with the 300 metres of boundary material, considering the parallel partition. (2)

So, {tex}A{/tex} is maximum at {tex}x=75{/tex} Also, maximum area is {tex}A=\frac{{7 5}}{{3}}({3 0 0}-{1 5 0})={3 7 5 0} {m}^{{2}}{/tex} OR {tex}A=\frac{x}{3}(300-2 x)=\frac{1}{3}\left(300 x-2 x^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d A}{d x}=\frac{1}{3}(300-4 x){/tex} For critical points, put {tex}\frac{d A}{d x}=0 \Rightarrow x=75{/tex} As {tex}\frac{d A}{d x}{/tex} changes its sign from positive to negative as {tex}x{/tex} passes through {tex}x=75{/tex} from left to right, which means {tex}x=75{/tex} is the point of maximum. Also, maximum area is {tex}A=\frac{{7 5}}{{3}}({3 0 0}-{1 5 0})={3 7 5 0} {m}^{{2}}{/tex} Note: Full credit to be given if the student takes equation as {tex}2 x+2 y=300{/tex} or {tex}2 x+4 y=300{/tex} or {tex}4 x+4 y=300{/tex} or {tex}4 x+3 y=300{/tex} The solutions of sub-parts will differ and marks may be given accordingly.

So, {tex}A{/tex} is maximum at {tex}x=75{/tex} Also, maximum area is {tex}A=\frac{{7 5}}{{3}}({3 0 0}-{1 5 0})={3 7 5 0} {m}^{{2}}{/tex} OR {tex}A=\frac{x}{3}(300-2 x)=\frac{1}{3}\left(300 x-2 x^{2}\right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d A}{d x}=\frac{1}{3}(300-4 x){/tex} For critical points, put {tex}\frac{d A}{d x}=0 \Rightarrow x=75{/tex} As {tex}\frac{d A}{d x}{/tex} changes its sign from positive to negative as {tex}x{/tex} passes through {tex}x=75{/tex} from left to right, which means {tex}x=75{/tex} is the point of maximum. Also, maximum area is {tex}A=\frac{{7 5}}{{3}}({3 0 0}-{1 5 0})={3 7 5 0} {m}^{{2}}{/tex} Note: Full credit to be given if the student takes equation as {tex}2 x+2 y=300{/tex} or {tex}2 x+4 y=300{/tex} or {tex}4 x+4 y=300{/tex} or {tex}4 x+3 y=300{/tex} The solutions of sub-parts will differ and marks may be given accordingly.

      {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{d A}{d x}=\frac{1}{3}(300-4 x){/tex}

      Also, {tex}\frac{d^{2} A}{d x^{2}}=-\frac{4}{3} \lt 0{/tex}. So, {tex}A{/tex} is maximum at {tex}x=75{/tex}
      Also, maximum area is {tex}A=\frac{{7 5}}{{3}}({3 0 0}-{1 5 0})={3 7 5 0} {m}^{{2}}{/tex}

question_text: A rectangular visiting card is to contain 24 sq.cm. of printed matter. The margins at the top and bottom of the card are to be 1 cm and the margins on the left and right are to be {tex}1 \frac{1}{2} {~cm}{/tex} as shown below : On the basis of the above information, answer the following questions : Write the expression for the area of the visiting card in terms of {tex}x{/tex} .     solving {tex}A^{\prime}(x)=0 \Rightarrow x=6{/tex} is the critical point. {tex}A^{\prime \prime}(6)=\frac{144}{6} \gt 0,{/tex} {tex} \therefore \text { Area of the card is minimum at } x=6, y=4{/tex}

question_text: Show that the right circular cylinder of given surface and maximum volume is such that its height is equal to the diameter of the base.
explanation: Let radius of the cylinder = r Height of the cylinder = h| {tex}s = 2\pi rh + 2\pi {r^2}{/tex} (1) {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{{s-2\pi {r^2}}}{{2\pi r}} = h{/tex} Now volume of cylinder is, {tex}v = \pi {r^2}h{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}v = \pi .{r^2}\left( {\frac{{s - 2\pi {r^2}}}{{2\pi r}}} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow v = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {sr - 2\pi {r^3}} \right]{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dr}} = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {s - 6\pi {r^2}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{r^2}}} = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {0 - 12\pi r} \right]{/tex} For maximum/minimum {tex}\frac{{dv}}{{dr}} = 0{/tex} {tex} \Rightarrow s = 6\pi {r^2}{/tex} From equ (1) {tex}\Rightarrow 2\pi rh + \ 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} {r^2} = 6\pi {r^2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow r = \frac{h}{2}{/tex} {tex}{\left[ {\frac{{{d^2}v}}{{d{r^2}}}} \right]_{r = \frac{h}{2}}} = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {0 - 12\pi \times \frac{h}{2}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = - 3\pi h < 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} s is maximum at {tex}r = \frac{h}{2}{/tex} Hence h = 2r

Hence h = 2r

question_html: A wire of length 28m is to be cut into two pieces. One of the pieces is to be made into a square and the other into a circle. What should be the length of the two pieces so that the combined areas of the square and the circle is minimum?
question_text: A wire of length 28m is to be cut into two pieces. One of the pieces is to be made into a square and the other into a circle. What should be the length of the two pieces so that the combined areas of the square and the circle is minimum?
explanation: Let 1 st length = x 2 nd length = 28 - x Now circumference of circle is 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}2\pi r = x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}r = \frac{x}{{2\pi }}{/tex} Now perimeter of rectangle = 4a {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}4a = 28 - x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow a = 7 - \frac{x}{4}{/tex} ATQ A = area of circle + area of square {tex}\pi {\left( {\frac{x}{{2\pi }}} \right)^2} + {\left( {7 - \frac{x}{4}} \right)^2}{/tex} Now, {tex}A = \pi .\frac{{{x^2}}}{{4{\pi ^2}}} + {\left( {7 - \frac{x}{4}} \right)^2}{/tex} So, {tex}\frac{{dA}}{{dx}} = \frac{{2x}}{{4\pi }} + 2\left( {7 - \frac{x}{4}} \right)\left( { - \frac{1}{4}} \right){/tex} {tex}\frac{{dA}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{{2x}}{{4\pi }} + 2\left( {7 - \frac{x}{4}} \right)\left( { - \frac{1}{4}} \right) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}\left( {7 - \frac{x}{4}} \right) = \frac{x}{{2\pi }}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}7 - \frac{x}{4} = \frac{x}{\pi }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}7 = \frac{x}{\pi } + \frac{x}{4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}7 = x\left( {\frac{{4 + \pi }}{{4\pi }}} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{{28\pi }}{{4 + \pi }} = x{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} = \frac{1}{{2\pi }} - \frac{1}{2}\left( {\frac{{ - 1}}{4}} \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{{2\pi }} + \frac{1}{8}{/tex} positive, hence minimum Therefore, 1 st length {tex}= \frac{{28\pi }}{{4 + \pi }}{/tex} 2 nd length {tex}= \frac{{28}}{1} - \frac{{28\pi }}{{4 + \pi }}{/tex} {tex}= 28\left[ {\frac{{4 + \pi - \pi }}{{4 + \pi }}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{112}}{{4 + \pi }}{/tex}
solution: Let 1 st length = x 2 nd length = 28 - x Now circumference of circle is 2 {tex}\pi{/tex} r {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}2\pi r = x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}r = \frac{x}{{2\pi }}{/tex} Now perimeter of rectangle = 4a {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}4a = 28 - x{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow a = 7 - \frac{x}{4}{/tex} ATQ A = area of circle + area of square {tex}\pi {\left( {\frac{x}{{2\pi }}} \right)^2} + {\left( {7 - \frac{x}{4}} \right)^2}{/tex} Now, {tex}A = \pi .\frac{{{x^2}}}{{4{\pi ^2}}} + {\left( {7 - \frac{x}{4}} \right)^2}{/tex} So, {tex}\frac{{dA}}{{dx}} = \frac{{2x}}{{4\pi }} + 2\left( {7 - \frac{x}{4}} \right)\left( { - \frac{1}{4}} \right){/tex} {tex}\frac{{dA}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{{2x}}{{4\pi }} + 2\left( {7 - \frac{x}{4}} \right)\left( { - \frac{1}{4}} \right) = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}\left( {7 - \frac{x}{4}} \right) = \frac{x}{{2\pi }}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}7 - \frac{x}{4} = \frac{x}{\pi }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}7 = \frac{x}{\pi } + \frac{x}{4}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}7 = x\left( {\frac{{4 + \pi }}{{4\pi }}} \right){/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{{28\pi }}{{4 + \pi }} = x{/tex} Now, {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} = \frac{1}{{2\pi }} - \frac{1}{2}\left( {\frac{{ - 1}}{4}} \right){/tex} {tex}= \frac{1}{{2\pi }} + \frac{1}{8}{/tex} positive, hence minimum Therefore, 1 st length {tex}= \frac{{28\pi }}{{4 + \pi }}{/tex} 2 nd length {tex}= \frac{{28}}{1} - \frac{{28\pi }}{{4 + \pi }}{/tex} {tex}= 28\left[ {\frac{{4 + \pi - \pi }}{{4 + \pi }}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \frac{{112}}{{4 + \pi }}{/tex}
solution_html:
Let 1st length = x
2nd length = 28 - x

Now circumference of circle is 2{tex}\pi{/tex}r
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}2\pi r = x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}r = \frac{x}{{2\pi }}{/tex} 
Now perimeter of rectangle = 4a
{tex}\therefore{/tex}{tex}4a = 28 - x{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow a = 7 - \frac{x}{4}{/tex} 
ATQ
A = area of circle + area of square
{tex}\pi {\left( {\frac{x}{{2\pi }}} \right)^2} + {\left( {7 - \frac{x}{4}} \right)^2}{/tex} 
Now, {tex}A = \pi .\frac{{{x^2}}}{{4{\pi ^2}}} + {\left( {7 - \frac{x}{4}} \right)^2}{/tex} 
So,{tex}\frac{{dA}}{{dx}} = \frac{{2x}}{{4\pi }} + 2\left( {7 - \frac{x}{4}} \right)\left( { - \frac{1}{4}} \right){/tex} 
{tex}\frac{{dA}}{{dx}} = 0{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\frac{{2x}}{{4\pi }} + 2\left( {7 - \frac{x}{4}} \right)\left( { - \frac{1}{4}} \right) = 0{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}\left( {7 - \frac{x}{4}} \right) = \frac{x}{{2\pi }}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}7 - \frac{x}{4} = \frac{x}{\pi }{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}7 = \frac{x}{\pi } + \frac{x}{4}{/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}7 = x\left( {\frac{{4 + \pi }}{{4\pi }}} \right){/tex} 
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}\frac{{28\pi }}{{4 + \pi }} = x{/tex} 
Now, {tex}\frac{{{d^2}y}}{{d{x^2}}} = \frac{1}{{2\pi }} - \frac{1}{2}\left( {\frac{{ - 1}}{4}} \right){/tex} 
{tex}= \frac{1}{{2\pi }} + \frac{1}{8}{/tex} 
positive, hence minimum
Therefore, 1st length {tex}= \frac{{28\pi }}{{4 + \pi }}{/tex} 
2nd length {tex}= \frac{{28}}{1} - \frac{{28\pi }}{{4 + \pi }}{/tex} 
{tex}= 28\left[ {\frac{{4 + \pi - \pi }}{{4 + \pi }}} \right]{/tex} 
{tex}= \frac{{112}}{{4 + \pi }}{/tex}
